order, impugned in this appeal.
9. It  is  directed  that  in  the  event  of  the
appellant being arrested, he shall be released on
bail on terms and conditions to be imposed by
the trial court.
10. Since the investigation is yet to conclude,
we  direct  that if the  investigating officer  calls
upon  the  appellant  to  continue  to  join  the
investigation, he  shall do so by attending  the
police station failing which liberty is granted to
bring any such omission to the notice of the trial
court and seek cancellation of bail.
11. Needless to observe, the appellant shall not,
directly  or  indirectly,  by  making  inducement,
threat  or  promise,  dissuade  any  person
acquainted  with  the  facts  of  the  case  from
disclosing such facts to any police officer or to
Crl. Appeal @  SLP (Crl.) No.5675/2026 3

--- Page 548 ---
the court.
12. We  clarify  that  the  observations  made  in
this order and grant of bail to the appellant in
anticipation  of  arrest  will  not  be  treated  as
findings on the merits of the case.
13. The appeal is, accordingly, allowed on the
aforesaid terms.
14. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  stand
disposed of.

THE STATE OF JHARKHAND            RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Leave granted.
3. The appellant seeks leave to challenge the
impugned  judgment  and  order  dated  19.02.2026
passed by the High Court of Jharkhand at Ranchi in
I.A. No.14609 of 2025 in Crl. Appeal(DB) No.2030
of 2023, titled  “Prem Munda Vs. The State of
Criminal Appeal @ Diary No.20402/2026                   1     

--- Page 552 ---
Jharkhand”, whereby the application for suspension
of sentence and grant of bail has been dismissed.
4. The  appellant  has  already  undergone  an
incarceration for a period of three years four
months (approximate) in connection with NDPS Case
No.23/2019  (Arising  out  of  Namkum  P.S.  Case
No.104/2019) on the file of the learned Additional
Judicial  Commissioner-XV,  Ranchi,  District  –
Ranchi, Jharkhand.
5. We have noticed the nature of crime and the
manner in which it was allegedly committed.
6. Having heard learned counsel for the parties,
and perused the material placed on record, we are
of the considered view that the appellant has made
out a case for interference with the impugned
judgment and order dated 19.02.2026.
7. Considering the totality of circumstances, we
are inclined to suspend the sentence imposed by
the Trial Court and grant bail to the appellant on
such terms and conditions as may be fixed by the
Trial Court, pending consideration of the appeal
Criminal Appeal @ Diary No.20402/2026                   2     

--- Page 553 ---
before the High Court. Ordered accordingly.
8. To comply with the order, the appellant shall
be produced before the Trial Court forthwith.
9. Hearing in the appeal, pending before the
High Court, expedited. Needless to add, appellant
shall not misuse the liberty in any manner. He
shall fully cooperate with the proceedings pending
before the High Court. It shall be open for the
High  Court  to  take  all  steps,  including  the
cancellation of bail, should the need so arises.
10. If the appellant possesses a passport, the
same shall be surrendered before the Competent
Authority/Court until the conclusion of the trial
or directed otherwise.
11. The  impugned  judgment  and  order  dated
19.02.2026 passed by the High Court of Jharkhand
at  Ranchi  in  I.A.  No.14609  of  2025  in  Crl.
Appeal(DB) No.2030 of 2023, titled “Prem Munda Vs.
The State of Jharkhand”, shall stand set aside.
12. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed.

      UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                    O R D E R
 
   IA No. 157326/2026
1. The present application has been filed on
behalf of National Legal Services Authority seeking
directions to those States/Union Territories which
have not yet furnished the nominations of their Home
Secretaries or their nominees to the High-Powered
Committee  in  terms  of  the  judgment  dated  26 th
February, 2026.
4

--- Page 561 ---
2. It  is  submitted  that,  despite  a  specific
direction to all the States/Union Territories, the
States of Andhra Pradesh, Assam, Chhattisgarh, Goa,
Karnataka, Kerala, Maharashtra, Manipur, Meghalaya,
Odisha,  Telangana  and  Uttar  Pradesh  are  yet  to
furnish  the  requisite  nominations.  Accordingly,
appropriate directions may be issued to the aforesaid
States/Union Territories to comply with the judgment
dated 26th February, 2026 and furnish the nominations
at the earliest.
3. During  the  course  of  hearing,  Ms.  Rashmi
Nandakumar, learned counsel appearing on behalf of
the  National  Legal  Services  Authority,  submitted
that, in the meantime, the States of Goa, Uttar
Pradesh and Chhattisgarh have furnished the details
of their respective nominees in compliance with the
judgment dated 26 th February, 2026. Accordingly, the
issue now survives only with respect to the remaining
States/Union Territories which are yet to furnish the
requisite nominations.
4. In view of the above, and considering that the
non-furnishing  of  the  requisite  nominations  is
impeding the constitution/functioning of the High-
Powered Committee and its ability to effectively
discharge the mandate entrusted to it under the
judgment dated 26 th February, 2026, the States of
5

--- Page 562 ---
Andhra  Pradesh,  Assam,  Karnataka,  Kerala,
Maharashtra, Manipur, Meghalaya, Odisha and Telangana
are mandated to furnish the details of their nominees
in terms of the said judgment at the earliest and,
preferably, within a period of one week from the date
of this order failing which, we shall be compelled to
direct coercive measures.
5. Insofar as the nomination of the Directors
General  of  Prisons  and  Correctional  Services  is
concerned, the same shall be made by the Executive
Chairperson  of  the  High-Powered  Committee  in
accordance with the aforesaid judgment.
6. The Registry is directed to transmit a copy of
this order to the learned Standing Counsel for the
States as well as to the Chief Secretaries of the
States of Andhra Pradesh, Assam, Karnataka, Kerala,
Maharashtra, Manipur, Meghalaya, Odisha and Telangana
for information and necessary compliance.
(SONIA BHASIN)
ASSISTANT REGISTRAR-CUM-PS
 (MANOJ KUMAR)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.            OF 2026
DIARY NO. 13394 OF 2026
SURJYOTI SUJALCHANDRA BAGCHI APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
BIMAL KUMAR AGARWAL & ANR. RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Admit.  The National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT), Mumbai
Bench, while approving the Resolution Plan under Section 30(6)
read with Section 31(1) of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code,
2016 (IBC) read with Regulation 39(4) of the Insolvency and
Bankruptcy Board of India (Insolvency Resolution Process for
Corporate Persons) Regulations, 2016 made it clear through
paragraph 12.12, as follows:
“12.12.  We also hereby make it clear that the
Employees Provident Fund dues of INR 24,83,544/- and
the Gratuity dues of INR 30,55,049/-, as provided by
the Applicant in the Annexure 'B' to the additional
affidavit dated 02.04.2025, being the statutory dues,
are required to be paid in full and the same shall
not be subject to the outer limit of INR 1 crores
earmarked by the SRA in the Resolution Plan for the
Operational Creditors including the Employees.”
1

--- Page 564 ---
2. The appellant is an employee representative of Vidarbha
Industries Private Limited, whose above-stated Resolution Plan
was approved by NCLT. She is aggrieved by the low sum of money
proposed to be paid to the employees pursuant to the aforesaid
Resolution Plan, namely, the total sum of Rs.1 crore.  The
salient features of the Resolution Plan as reproduced in
paragraph 17 of the impugned order dated 16.01.2026, passed by
the National Company Law Appellate Tribunal (NCLAT), are to
the following effect:
“17. Now coming to the submissions raised by the
learned counsel for the appellant in Comp. App. (AT)
(Ins.) No. 1317/2025 which is an appeal filed by one
employee  claiming  to  be  representative  of  the
employees challenging the approval of the resolution
plan. The impugned order has noticed the amount
claimed and amount admitted of different stakeholders
and the amount proposed in the resolution plan.
Paragraph 6.1 of the impugned order captures the
salient  features  of  plan  approved  by  the  CoC.
Paragraph 6.1 is as follows:
“6.1 The SRA has proposed a Total Resolution Amount
of INR 4000,00,00,000/- (Rupees Four Thousand Crores
only) for the resolution of the Corporate Debtor in
the following manner:
2

--- Page 565 ---
Sr.

3. In the peculiar facts and circumstances of this case, it
seems to us that paragraph 12.12 of the order passed by the
NCLT deserves to be read to mean that the amount of Rs. 1
crore provided under the Resolution Plan to the operational
creditors where employees have the first preferential claim,
shall be in addition to and over and above the amount of
employees’ provident fund and gratuity, as quantified by the
NCLT.  The additional amount, as a consequence thereof, shall
be paid by the successful resolution applicant within a period
of two weeks. Barring this addition, we find no error in the
approval of the Resolution Plan.
3

--- Page 566 ---
4. The appeal stands disposed of in the above terms.

CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO(S).          OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRL.) NO(S). 593 OF 2026)
BHAGWAT SAH                                   APPELLANT
VERSUS
THE STATE OF BIHAR                           RESPONDENT
O R D E R
Leave granted.
This  appeal  challenges  the  order  dated
28.11.2025 passed by the High Court of Judicature
at  Patna  in  Criminal  Miscellaneous  No.
73068/2025. 
Apprehending  arrest  in  connection  with
crime registered pursuant to F.I.R No.163 of 2021
lodged with Jogapatti Police Station, District
Bettiah in respect of the offences punishable
under Sections 341, 323, 324, 307, 354, 379, 504
read with 34 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 [in
short,  “IPC”],  the  appellant  preferred  an
1

--- Page 570 ---
application  before  the  High  Court  seeking
anticipatory bail in terms of Section 438 of the
Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 [for short,
“CrPC”].
Said application for anticipatory bail has
been rejected by the High Court  vide  impugned
order dated 28.11.2025. Hence the instant appeal
has been preferred.
This  Court  vide  order  dated  15.01.2026
issued notice and granted interim protection from
arrest to the appellant.
We  have  heard  learned  counsel  for  the
appellant in support of the appeal and learned
counsel for the respondent-state. 
Learned counsel for the appellant submitted
that the name of the appellant does not figure in
the charge sheet; however, the learned Trial
Judge has taken cognizance of his role in the
incident  and  notice  has  been  issued  to  the
appellant to participate in the trial as an
accused.  Learned  counsel  for  the  appellant
2

--- Page 571 ---
further submitted that this Court by order dated
15.01.2026 had granted interim protection and the
same may be made absolute subject to the terms
and conditions to be imposed.
Per contra , learned standing counsel for
the  respondent-State  with  reference  to  the
counter-affidavit  contended  that  there  is  no
merit  in  this  appeal  and  the  same  may  be
dismissed.
Considering the circumstances on record, in
our view, the appellant is entitled to the relief
claimed under Section 438 of the CrPC.
We, therefore, allow this appeal and set
aside the order passed by the High Court dated
28.11.2025. 
We direct that in the event of arrest of
the  appellant,  the  Arresting  Officer  shall
release  the  appellant  on  bail  subject  to
furnishing  cash  security  in  the  sum  of
Rs.25,000/- (Rupees Twenty-Five Thousand only)
with two like sureties.
3

--- Page 572 ---
It is directed that the appellant shall
extend complete cooperation in the ensuing trial.
The appellant shall not misuse his liberty
and shall not in any way influence the witnesses
or tamper with the material on record.
With the aforesaid directions, the appeal
is allowed.
………………………………………………………,J
(B.V. NAGARATHNA)
          ………………………………………………………,J
      (UJJAL BHUYAN)
NEW DELHI;
MAY 26, 2026

                             O R D E R
IA No. 168742/2025:
This application has been filed by petitioner no.5
(Sobhdar, S/o Abhan Khan) praying that the matter be
referred to the Trial Court for conducting an inquiry
with regard to whether he was a juvenile on the date of
incident. Certain documents have been brought on record
and response was sought from the respondent no.1-State
of Rajasthan. Initially, an affidavit was filed, which
the  Court  found  to  be  wanting  and  thus,  the
Superintendent of Police, District Barmer, Rajasthan,
was  called  to  be  personally  present  along  with  an
affidavit. Today, Mr. Chunaram Jat, IPS, Superintendent
of Police, District Barmer, Rajasthan, is present. In
the affidavit filed by him, stand taken is that the
documents have been verified by the Police from the
concerned authorities. 
2. In view of the aforesaid, we refer the matter to the
2

--- Page 577 ---
SLP (CRL.)  NO. 13510/2025
Additional  Sessions  Judge,  Barmer,  Rajasthan,  in
Sessions Case No. 2 of 1993 to inquire and submit a
report with respect to the age of petitioner no.5 in
terms of Section 7A of the Juvenile Justice (Care and
Protection of Children) Act, 2000, which is in  pari
materia to section 9 of the Juvenile Justice (Care and
Protection of Children) Act, 2015 (which was in force at
the relevant point of time). The report be submitted to
this Court within three months from today.
4. IA  No.  168742/2025  stands  disposed  of  in  the
aforementioned terms.
IA No. 63843/2026:
Learned counsel for the petitioners submits that the
present application has been filed seeking suspension of
sentence  and  grant  of  bail  to  the  petitioner  no.5
(Sobhdar, S/o Abhan Khan) during the pendency of the
present proceedings. 
2. It was submitted that the petitioner no.5 coming
from rural background and the guardians not being aware
and father of the petitioner no.5 being made an accused,
who died during the pendency of the appeal before the
High Court, the petitioner no.5 was unable to place
3

--- Page 578 ---
SLP (CRL.)  NO. 13510/2025
before the Court the fact that he was a juvenile on the
date of the incident. It was submitted that in view of
the certificate, which has been brought on record by
him, which on verification has been found to be in
order, the Court may consider suspending his sentence
and release him on bail. It was further contended that
he was not even charge-sheeted and only during the trial
he  was  summoned  under  Section  319  of  the  Code  of
Criminal  Procedure,  1973  and  convicted  and  in  the
appeal, his conviction has been upheld.
3. It  was  submitted  that  during  the  trial  he  was
released within one month of his arrest and even during
the appeal before the High Court, he was on bail.
Further,  it  was  submitted  that  as  of  now  he  has
completed more than 1½ years in custody. It was further
contended that there is no allegation by any of the
witnesses with regard to any direct overt act by him and
only with the aid of Section 149 of the Indian Penal
Code, 1860, he was convicted. It was further contended
that initially the witnesses also stated that he was not
present, but later on in their evidence before the
Court, the story has been developed and he was also
named.
4

--- Page 579 ---
SLP (CRL.)  NO. 13510/2025
4. Learned counsel for the State of Rajasthan submitted
that the Court has recorded the facts and has referred
the matter for inquiry. However, it was contended that
upon trial, petitioner no.5 has also been convicted.
5. Learned counsel for the respondent no.2 has also
vehemently opposed the prayer for bail. 
6. Having considered the matter in its entirety, we are
inclined to allow the prayer for suspension of sentence
and grant of bail to the petitioner no.5. Accordingly,
petitioner no.5 is directed to be released on bail
subject to conditions as may be imposed by the Trial
Court.
7. IA  No.  63843/2026  stands  disposed  of  in  the
aforementioned terms.
8. List the main petition for hearing on 11.8.2026.
  (POOJA SHARMA)                            (ANJALI PANWAR)
    AR-CUM-PS                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR
5

--- Page 580 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.      /2026
@ SLP (CRL) NO.  953/2026
NARENDRA @ GUDDU ..... APPELLANT
              VERSUS
STATE OF MAHARASHTRA & ANR. ..... RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Leave granted.
3. This  Court  while  issuing  notice  on  the
present appeal on 06.01.2026 had restricted the
same only with regard to issue of sentence.
4. Having heard the learned counsels appearing
for the parties and on perusal of the records, it
could emerge therefrom that appellant was tried
for the offences punishable under Sections 354-
A(1)(i), 363, 506 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860
(“IPC”)  and  Section  12  of  the  Protection  of
Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 (“POCSO
Act”) and was convicted and sentenced to undergo 1
1

--- Page 581 ---
year, 6 years, 2 years and 3 years rigorous
imprisonment  for  respective  offences  which
sentences were ordered to run concurrently. The
High Court by the impugned order maintained the
conviction  and  sentence  for  the  offences
punishable under Sections 363 and 506 of the IPC
but set aside the conviction and sentence imposed
for the offences punishable under Section 354-A(1)
(i) of the IPC and Section 12 of the POCSO Act and
acquitted the appellant for the said offences.
Hence, this appeal.
5. Having heard the learned counsels appearing
for the parties, we notice that appellant has
undergone  2  years  8  months  and  7  days  of
imprisonment as on 07.09.2025 which would be 3
years 4 months as on date. There are no adverse
report  from  the  Jail  Superintendent  against
appellant. 
6. Having  regard  to  the  totality  of
circumstances  of  the  case,  we  are  of  the
considered view that judgment of conviction would
2

--- Page 582 ---
not warrant our interference. However, with regard
to the sentence, we notice that appellant has
undergone substantial period of sentence of more
than three years and four months as against six
years imposed and there being no adverse report
while in jail, we are of the considered view that
sentence requires to be modified and we modify the
sentence  to  the  period  already  undergone  and
accordingly, allow this appeal in part and direct
appellant to be released forthwith, if he is not
undergoing imprisonment in other cases or being
required in any other case.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.
     ..................J.
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
..................J.
(PRASANNA B. VARALE)
NEW DELHI;
MAY 22, 2026.

PREETI SOMY SARAN & ORS.                           Petitioner(s)

SHETH AMICHAND PANNALAL ADISHWAR JAIN TEMPLE

(IA No. 93186/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED
JUDGMENT  IA  No.  94344/2026  -  PERMISSION  TO  FILE  ADDITIONAL
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES)
 
Date : 18-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.K. MAHESHWARI
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Sajan Narain, Adv.
                   Mr. Rajan Narain, AOR                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Sushil Kumar Jain, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Shivaji M. Jadhav, AOR

                   Mr. Brij Kishor Sah, Adv.

                   Mr. Siddharth Jain, Adv.
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The present special leave petition is filed by the petitioner
being aggrieved by the judgment and order dated 29.1.2026 passed in
Writ Petition No. 4051 of 1996 maintaining the order of eviction of
the Rent Controlling Authority, directing the ejectment of the

--- Page 594 ---
2
tenant.  When the matter came up for hearing on 24.04.2026, we have
passed the order as under :
“1)  After  hearing  learned  counsel  for  the
parties a length, this matter is proposed to be
disposed of on a happy note of settlement
between the parties. The primary terms are that
if the respondent pays Rs.3 crores on deduction
of  TDS,  the  petitioner  shall  vacate  the
premises on or before 31.07.2026.
2) After deduction of TDS, Demand Draft be
brought within two weeks before the Court which
shall  be  kept  in  Fixed  Deposit  with  the
Registry. On producing the documents before the
Registrar of vacating the premises on or before
31.07.2026, the amount lying with the Registry
be immediately transmitted along with accrued
interest, if any. Affidavit along with PAN
card, AADHAR card and Account details be filed
within two weeks.
3) Subject to the aforesaid payment and handing
over of possession of the entire premises, this
litigation may be put to quietus by the orders
of the Court.
4) List on 11th May, 2026.”
2. In furtherance, it was decided that if the respondent pays a
sum of Rs.3,00,00,000/- (Rupees three crore only), the tenant shall
vacate the premises on or before 31.07. 2026.  The amount of
Rs.3,00,00,000/- has been deposited in the following manner :
Sr. No. Demand Draft No. Amount
1 844188 Rs.99,00,000/-
2. 844189 Rs.99,00,000/-
3. 844190 Rs.47,68,000/-

--- Page 595 ---
3
3. In addition, as per Office Report dated 16.05.2026, a sum of
Rs.54,32,000/- was deposited by way of Demand Draft No.844194
making a total amount of Rs.3,00,00,000/-.  The amounts deposited
was kept in an interest bearing short term fixed deposit in a
nationalised bank.
4. It was informed that a sum of Rs.54,32,000/- has further been
deposited with the Income Tax Authorities towards TDS, Cess and
surcharge.  But , since the petitioner was not a non-resident, the
income tax is not payable.  Therefore, the said amount was required
to be refunded to the respondents by the income-tax authorities on
taking steps by the respondent.
5. In view of the order passed on 24.04.2026 and in furtherance
of the compliance reported, we direct as under :
(i) The petitioner shall vacate the entire suit premises on or
before 31.07.2026 and handing over the keys before the Trial
Court concerned to the respondent to which a report be
immediately sent to the Registry of this Court.
(ii) On receiving the report, the amount of Rs.3,00,00,000/- lying
with the Registry of this Court shall be transmitted in the
account of the petitioner along with accrued. The account
details be provided by the petitioner to the Registry to
facilitate the transmission of the account.

--- Page 596 ---
4
(iii) The amount of Rs.54,32,000/- deposited as TDS, Cess and
Surcharge with the Income-tax Authorities shall be refunded
to the respondents subject to verification.
6. In view of the aforesaid, the present special leave petitions
stand disposed of.  Pending applications, if any, shall also stand
disposed of.
(GULSHAN KUMAR ARORA)                           (NAND KISHOR)

Miscellaneous Application No.  1546/2026 in SLP(C) No. 22173/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  10-12-2025
in SLP(C) No. 22173/2025 passed by the Supreme Court of India]
S.N. TRADING COMPANY & ANR.                        Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
UNION OF INDIA & ORS.                              Respondent(s)
 
WITH
MA 1547/2026 in SLP(C) No. 22144/2025 (XVI)
Date : 26-05-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIJAY BISHNOI
For Petitioner(s) :  By Courts Motion, AOR
                   
                   Mr. Vivek Sarin, Adv.
                   Mr. Aakarshan Aditya, AOR
                   Ms. Divyanshi Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Satish C. Kaushik, Adv.

For Respondent(s) :Mr. N Venkataraman, A.S.G.
                   Mr. Gurmeet Singh Makker, AOR
                   Mr. V C Bharathi, Adv.
                   Mr. Bhakti Vardhan Singh, Adv.
1

--- Page 606 ---
M.A. No.1546/2026 in SLP(C) No. 22173/2025
                   Mr. Piyush Beriwal, Adv.
                   Mr. P V Yogeswaran, Adv.
                   Mrs. Meera Patel, Adv.
                   
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Our order dated 15.5.2026 reads thus:-
“1. Our order dated 11.08.2025 reads thus:-
“1.  Heard  the  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the
petitioners.
2. Exemption Applications are allowed.
3.  Subject  to  deposit  of  Rs.5,98,000/-  with  the
Registry of this Court in each matter, issue notice to
the respondents.
4. Dasti service, in addition, is permitted.
5. The learned counsel appearing for the petitioners
submitted  that  within  a  week  from  today,  the
petitioners will deposit the amount of Rs.5,98,000/- in
each matter with the Registry of this Court.
6.  No  sooner  the  amount  is  deposited  by  the
petitioners, then the matter shall be notified for
further hearing.
7. Since a statement has been made and the petitioners
have agreed to deposit the amount, referred to above,
within a period of one week, the department shall not
proceed with the auction of the goods i.e. areca nuts.
This amount of Rs.5,98,000/- which we have asked the
petitioners to deposit is the amount which has been
imposed upon the petitioners by way of penalty.”
2. Thereafter, on 10.12.2025, we passed the following order:-
“1. Having heard the learned counsel appearing for the
petitioners and having gone through the materials on
record, we find no good ground to interfere with the
impugned orders passed by the High Court. 
2.  The  Special  Leave  Petitions  are,  accordingly,
dismissed.
3. Pending applications, if any, also stand disposed
of.”
3. In  pursuance  of  our  order  dated  11.08.2025,  the
original petitioners deposited the amount of Rs.5,98,000/-
with the Registry of this Court.
2

--- Page 607 ---
M.A. No.1546/2026 in SLP(C) No. 22173/2025
4. The Registry wants this Court to clarify what has to be
done with this amount of Rs.5,98,000/- deposited by the
petitioners because the Special Leave Petition has already
been dismissed vide order dated 10.12.2025 referred to
above.
5. Post  these  Miscellaneous  Applications  on
26.05.2026.
6. On the next date of hearing, a responsible person
from the Company shall personally remain present before
this Court.
7. Registry  to  inform  about  this  order  to  the
petitioners.”
2. In pursuance of our order, referred to above, Mr. Vinod Maurya
and Pramod Maurya respectively claiming to be the owners of the
company are personally present before this Court.
3. As of abundant caution, we direct the Registry to transfer
this  amount  of  Rs.11,96,000/-(Rupees  Eleven  lakh  Ninety  Six
thousand only) with interest accrued thereon,  deposited by the
petitioners, to the office of the ACAO, CGST and Central Excise,
Siliguri Commissionerate at the earliest.
4. The Registry shall transfer this amount of Rs.11,96,000/-
(Rupees Eleven lakh Ninety Six thousand only) with interest accrued
thereon, if any, to the account of ACAO CGST and Central Excise
Siliguri Commissionerate, Account No. 44497072368, State Bank of
India, IFSC Code-SBIN0000184.
5. We take notice of the fact that Rs.5,98,000/- (Rupees Five

Leave granted.

4. Accused Nos.1 and 2 were apprehended near KK Grand
Hotel, Vijayawada in the presence of mediators and they
were found to be in conscious possession of 5.50 grams
of MDMA seized from Accused No.1 and 7.84 grams from
Accused No.2 in all 13.34 grams. Accused Nos.3, 4 and 5
are identified as absconding. An FIR No.443 of 2025 came
to be registered at Machavaram Police Station for the
offence punishable under Sections 8(c) read with 22(c)
of the NDPS Act. After, about fifteen days a newspaper
article titled  “Greeshma Kosam Gaalimpu”  published in

--- Page 610 ---
2
Eenadu,  Telugu  Daily  Amravati  Edition  alleging  that
Accused No.4-Mahesh used to purchase MDMA from a person
name  Greeshma,  claiming both allegedly studied in the
same University at Bengaluru, Karanataka and based on
his statement and the paper report the appellant was
implicated and apprehending her arrest she approached
the Trial Court as well as the High Court for grant of
anticipatory bail and same has been rejected. Hence,
appellant is before this Court.
5.  Having heard the learned Advocates appearing for
the parties and on perusal of the records, it would not
detain this Court for too long to accept the prayer of
the appellant for the simple reason that appellant has
been implicated based on the co-accused statement. As to
what is the complicity of the appellant, including her
role is an issue which shall have to be evaluated after
considering the evidence that may be  tendered by the
prosecution. Hence, we allow this appeal and set aside
the impugned order and direct that the appellant be
released  on  anticipatory  bail  by  the  Investigation
Officer on such terms and conditions as he deems fit,
including the condition of directing the appellant to
cooperate with the investigation and appear before the
Investigation Officer as and when called upon and shall
also appear before the Trial Court on all dates of
hearing unless exempted. It is made clear that in the
event  of  any  of  the  condition  imposed  by  the

--- Page 611 ---
3
Investigation Officer or by this Court is violated, the
prosecution would be at liberty to seek for cancellation
of bail. In the event of such an application being
filed, the Trial Court shall consider the same on its
own merits and in accordance with law.
6. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO.2185 OF 2025
RAGINI MISHRA   ..... PETITIONER
              VERSUS
UTPAL RANJAN  ..... RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. This petition has been filed for transfer of divorce petition
filed under Section 13(1)(ia) of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 by
respondent-husband.
2. As there was possibility of settlement, this Court referred the
matter to Supreme Court Mediation Centre, pursuant to which the
parties appeared before the Mediation Centre and after several
rounds  of  deliberations,  the  parties  have  entered  into  a
settlement. Same has been forwarded along with a report by the
Mediation Centre. The agreed terms of the settlement are as under:
“5. Both the parties hereto have arrived at an amicable settlement on
the following terms and conditions  for dissolution of marriage by
mutual consent:-
A. That the parties have agreed that the Respondent-husband will
pay  to  the  Petitioner-wife  a  total  sum  of  Rs.  27,50,000/-  (Rupees
Twenty Seven Lakhs and Fifty Thousand Only) towards full and final
settlement  of  all  her  claims  towards  her  permanent  alimony,
Stridhan, maintenance (past, present, future) and any other claim
whatsoever. 
B. That the Respondent/Husband shall return all ornaments/ items to
the Petitioner/wife as per the list below:-
a. 4 Gold Finger Rings 
b. 1 Gold Chain 
1

--- Page 614 ---
c. 1 Gold Bracelet
d. 1 Silver Bowl
e. 1 Juicer 
f. Clothes and Personal Accessories
C. It is agreed between the parties that they will move an application
jointly  before  the  Hon'ble  Supreme  Court  under  Article  142  of
Constitution  of  India  for  invoking  inherent  powers  of  the  Hon'ble
Supreme Court praying for divorce by mutual consent as parties are
staying separately since September 2023 i.e. for the last more than two
years and there is irretrievable break down of the marriage. 
D. It is further agreed between the parties that if Hon'ble Supreme
Court is pleased to allow application filed by both the parties under
Article 142 of Constitution of India then both the parties shall withdraw
all  the  cases  filed  by  them  against  each  other.  The  Respondent  -
husband will pay to the Petitioner/ Wife a total sum of Rs. 27,50,000/-
(Rupees  Twenty  Seven  Lakhs  and  Fifty  Thousand  Only)  via
RTGS/NEFT  /Online  Transfer/DD  in  the  account  Bearing  No.
50100570841733,  IFSC  Code.  HDFC0000239,  Drawn  On  HDFC  Bank,
Powai Hiranandani Branch, Mumbai, on or before 01st May 2026.
6. That the Petitioner and the Respondent have agreed that none of
them will initiate any other legal action or complaint against each other
or  against  the  family  members  of  each  other  in  respect  of  their
matrimonial dispute in future.
7. That subject to the aforesaid terms, the parties have resolved all the
disputes amicably in relation to the marriage and have been left with
no claims against each other or their respective family members.
8. That by signing this Agreement the parties hereto solemnly state
and affirm that they have no further claims or demands against each
other including maintenance, or any movable or immovable property
and all the disputes and differences have been amicably settled by the
parties hereto through the process of mediation. 
9. The parties undertake to abide by the terms and conditions set out
in  the  above  mentioned  Settlement  Agreement,  which  have  been
arrived with free will of the parties without any coercion, duress or
collusion and parties undertake not to raise any dispute whatsoever
henceforth.”
3. Both parties are present physically and they admit the execution
of the agreement. They have also stated that out of their own free
2

--- Page 615 ---
will without any force, threat or coercion, they have entered into
the settlement, after having understood the terms of settlement and
they  have  affixed  their  signatures  voluntarily.  The  learned
counsels appearing for the parties, in token of having identified
the parties present before the court, have also affixed their
signatures. This court has also interacted with the parties and
they have reiterated the terms set out in the settlement agreement.
4. In the light of the said settlement arrived at between the
parties and both being well educated and having understood the
consequences of the settlement agreement, this Court does not find
any impediment to accept the same and accordingly it stands
accepted  and  the  marriage  solemnized  between  the  parties  on
09.03.2023 stands dissolved as there is no possibility of re-union.
The parties also agree that there are no further claims against
each other.
5.Registry to draw the decree accordingly. Parties to bear their
respective costs. 
6. The Transfer Petition is disposed of.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

ITEM NO.31          COURT NO.14               SECTION XI
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
PETITION FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (C)  NO.  18465/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated
16-02-2026 in SA No. 219/2025 passed by the High Court of
Judicature at Allahabad]
BANARAS HINDU UNIVERSITY & ORS.           Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
DIPANWITA SINGH ROY & ORS.                Respondent(s)
IA No.160128/2026-EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED
JUDGMENT,  IA  No.  160126/2026  -  PERMISSION  TO  FILE
ADDITIONAL DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing 
today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANOJ MISRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Tushar Mehta, Solicitor General
                   Mr. Sameer Jain, Adv.
                   Mr. Himesh Thakur, Adv.
                   Mr. Bhuvan Kapoor, Adv.
                   Mr. Syed Fazl Askari, Adv.
                   Ms. Savi Garga, Adv.
                   Mr. Tenzen Tashi Negi, AOR
                                     
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Rajiv Dutta, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Vinayak Mohan, Adv.
                   Mr. Kedar Nath Tripathy, AOR
                   
                  
   UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The Division Bench of the High Court by the impugned
order reversed the decision of the learned Single Judge and
1

--- Page 618 ---
held the selection bad inter alia on the following grounds:
(a) There was no necessity for the Vice Chancellor to
invoke the emergency powers under Section 7C (5) of
the Banaras Hindu University Act (BHU Act);
(b) The Selection Committee apart from other members
required the presence of three experts having special
knowledge  in  the  subject  concerned.  The  subject
concerned was Dance (Kathak) whereas the expert, who
participated in the selection process did not have
expertise  in Kathak; 
and
(c)  Expert nominated to the Selection Committee by
the Vice-Chancellor was one amongst those forwarded by
the second respondent who was one of the aspiring
candidates,  therefore,  the  composition  of  the
Selection Committee is affected by the rule against
bias.
2. The submission on behalf of the appellant is that as
far as the invocation of emergency powers by the Vice-
Chancellor under Section 7C (5) of the Act is concerned,
there appears a direction of a coordinate Bench of the High
Court wherein, in the context of some other case relating
to appointment, it was observed that in the facts and
circumstances of the case particularly when the executive
2

--- Page 619 ---
council had not been constituted and its term has expired,
invocation of emergency powers could not be faulted. In
such circumstances, the view taken by the Division Bench
qua exercise of emergency powers is not warranted. 
3. On the second ground, the submission on behalf of the
petitioner is that subject post code was Dance without
specifying Kathak, Bharatnatyam or any other form of dance.
In such circumstances, merely because the two participants
were of Kathak stream, the composition of the Selection
Committee cannot be faulted. 
4. As far as the third ground taken by the High Court is
concerned, it is the case of the petitioner that under
Statute 11 A (3) of the University, the Policy and Planning
Committee is required to recommend the list of experts to
the Vice-Chancellor. The composition of that Committee,
relatable to a small department, like the department in
question, is as follows:
(i) The past Head of the department preceding the 
present Head;
(ii) The present Head of the department (convener);
(iii) The teacher likely to be appointed as the next 
Head after the expiry of the term of the present Head.
5. In that context, it has been submitted that the
presence of the second respondent in the Committee, which
3

--- Page 620 ---
recommends a panel of subject experts, was by way of
necessity. Besides, the Committee had recommended a panel
of 12 experts out of which one was nominated by the Vice-
Chancellor. 
6. In addition to above, it has been submitted that the
writ petitioner herself had appeared before the Selection
Committee on 04.01.2024 without raising any objection as to
its composition. Only on 01.03.2024 that a representation
was submitted in respect of composition of the Selection
Committee. Thus, according to the petitioner, the grounds
on which the High Court had set aside the selection are not
sustainable in law.
7. The matter requires consideration.
8. Issue notice.
9. Notice on behalf of respondent has been accepted by
Mr. Kedar Nath Tripathy, Advocate-on-Record.
10. List this matter on 29.07.2026.
11. In the meantime, the effect and operation of the
impugned order passed by the Division Bench of the High
Court shall remain stayed.
  (KAVITA PAHUJA)                          (SAPNA BANSAL)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                  COURT MASTER (NSH)
4

--- Page 621 ---
1

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.        /2026
(arising out of SLP(C) NO. 17910/2026)
PREMAL PRATAP JOISHER & ANR. APPELLANT(S)
                VERSUS
SHRI JYOTHIRAADHITYA INFRA & ORS. RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted. 
2. As the contesting parties are before this Court and are
agreeable, this matter is being disposed of today.
3. The challenge in this appeal is to the order dated 07.04.2026
passed by a Division Bench of the High Court of Andhra Pradesh
in Writ Appeal No. 285/2026. 
4. The said appeal was filed against the order dated 22.04.2025
passed by a learned Judge in Writ Petition No. 8881/2025. By
the  said  order,  the  learned  Judge  took  note  of  the
unconditional apology tendered by the Tahsildar, Somandepalli
Village and Mandal, Sri Satya Sai District, in relation to the
impugned notice issued by him on 15.03.2025, despite the
matter being  sub judice . The learned Judge also noted the
acknowledgement by the Tahsildar and his counsel that such
action on his part was without jurisdiction. The learned
1

--- Page 626 ---
Judge, accordingly, quashed the said notice and allowed the
writ petition. 
5. Surprisingly, by the impugned order dated 07.04.2026, the
Division  Bench  restored  the  said  notice  and  directed
appropriate action to be taken thereon. 
6. We may note that this Court had occasion to deal with an
offshoot  of  this  litigation  in  Civil  Appeal  Nos.  4577-
4578/2026,  which  were  disposed  of  by  judgment  dated
17.04.2027. This Court made it clear therein that, even if the
appellate authority under the A ndhra Pradesh Rights in Land
and Pattadar Pass Books Act, 1971, deals with the appeal
pending before him on its merits and in accordance with law,
the said authority should be mindful of the fact that the
substantial  appeal  in  A.S.  No.  101/2022  was  pending
consideration before the High Court and that any decision
rendered by the said authority would be subject to and would
have to abide by the final decision in the said first appeal.
It was, however, left open to the appellate authority to pass
appropriate orders with regard to the entries to be made in
the Revenue records, after hearing all the parties concerned,
on merits and in accordance with law. 
7. This being the situation, it was not open to the Tahsildar,
Somandepalli Village and Mandal, Sri Satya Sai District, to
initiate a fresh inquiry into the documents sought to be
relied  upon  by  the  appellants  under  threat  of  criminal
prosecution. The learned judge was, therefore, fully justified
2

--- Page 627 ---
in quashing the notice issued by the said Tahsildar, upon
accepting his apology and his acknowledgment that he had no
jurisdiction to issue such a notice. 
8. The Division Bench, therefore, erred in reversing the said
order and restoring the notice for further action to be taken
thereon. 
9. The appeal is accordingly allowed, setting aside the impugned
order dated 07.04.2026 passed by the Division Bench of the
High Court of Andhra Pradesh in Writ Appeal No. 285/2026. 
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
..........................J.
(SANJAY KUMAR)
..........................J.
(K. VINOD CHANDRAN)
NEW DELHI;
MAY 25, 2026.
3

--- Page 628 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.  1592/2025  
RAM BINOD JHA ..... APPELLANT
              VERSUS
PRIYA MISHRA ..... RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. IA No.146672/2026 is allowed. Petitioner to
carry out amendment of the cause-title by filing an
amended cause-title. 
3. The matrimonial dispute which has blossomed
itself in filing a petition for maintenance by the
wife and same has resulted in trial court awarding a
sum of Rs.30,000/- monthly maintenance to the wife
from  the  date  of  application  which  order  was
assailed by her before the High Court resulting in
enhancement to Rs.50,000/- and the High Court by the
impugned order has clarified in paragraph No.25 that
husband will start paying the monthly maintenance of
1

--- Page 630 ---
Rs.50,000/-  per  month  from  now  onwards,  i.e.,
September, 2023. Even in paragraph No.24, it has
been observed by the High Court that the monthly
maintenance of Rs.50,000/- per month shall be paid
in  the  first  week  of  every  succeeding  month.
However, there is no specific or clear order as to
the arrears and the opinion expressed by the High
Court with regard to arrears seems to be shrouded
with doubt, we say so for the reason as it has been
observed by the High Court under the impugned order
that arrears of amount of maintenance is concerned,
same should be calculated at Rs.50,000/- per month
from the date of filing till date. This ambiguity is
conspicuous.  Even  otherwise,  in  view  of  the
subsequent development that has taken place, namely,
the respondent – wife having expired in August, 2025
and both the children being major and having settled
down abroad, we deem it proper to put a quietus to
this litigation by clarifying and making it clear
that maintenance payable shall be @ Rs.50,000/- per
month from the date of the order passed by the High
Court, i.e., 11.08.2023. Thus, arrears payable would
be 24 months X Rs.20,000/- = Rs.4,80,000/-. The said
amount shall be deposited by the petitioner or paid
2

--- Page 631 ---
directly  to  the  legal  heirs  of  the  deceased
respondent – wife in equal proportion to them within
a period of four weeks from today. The appeal is
allowed in part. 
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.
     ..................J.
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
..................J.
(PRASANNA B. VARALE)
NEW DELHI;
MAY 22, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION NO.    /2026
(@ Diary No. 20484/2026)
in
CIVIL APPEAL NO. 67/2026
DIVJOT SEKHON  APPELLANT(S)
                   VERSUS
STATE OF PUNJAB & ORS. RESPONDENT(S)
IN THE MATTER OF:
MANSIRAT KAUR APPLICANT(S)
O R D E R
1. This miscellaneous application was filed by Mansirat Kaur,
respondent No. 5 in Civil Appeal No. 67/2026, with the following
prayer.
“Interchange/modify  the  sequence  in  which  the
Respondent No. 4 and 5 have been mentioned in the
judgment, in particular Para No. 40, to reflect their
inter se sports and academic; and 
Direction the Respondent Nos. 1-3 to issue a fresh
merit list in light of the clarification and place
the applicant/Respondent No. 5 Ms. Mansirat Kaur
higher in rank in comparison to Respondent No. 4 Ms.
Kudrat Kashyap and consequently award the Applicant a
Semi Government seat instead of a Management Seat.”.
1

--- Page 634 ---
2. However, we may note that, in paragraph 40 of the judgment
dated 06.01.2026 passed in Civil Appeal Nos. 67/2026, 68/2026 and
69/2026,  this  Court  merely  stated  that  Divjot  Sekhon  and
Shubhkarman Singh should be accommodated in the seats which were
allotted to Kudrat Kashyap and Mansirat Kaur and, in turn, Kudrat
Kashyap and Mansirat Kaur should be given the seats vacated by
Divjot Sekhon and Shubhkarman Singh.
3. This Court did not go into the issue as to the inter se merit
between Kudrat Kashyap and Mansirat Kaur, be it in terms of their
comparative sports achieveme nts under the State’s policy or even
otherwise. In the event any order has been passed in that regard,
it is for the party aggrieved thereby to challenge the same before
the appropriate forum in accordance with law.
4. Making this position clear, the miscellaneous application is
disposed of.
5. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
..........................J.
(SANJAY KUMAR)
..........................J.
(K. VINOD CHANDRAN)

CRIMINAL APPEAL NO. 2748 OF 2026
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO. 2748 OF 2026
(@ SLP (Criminal)  NO.6463/2026)
DINESH PRASAD APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
NAGESHWAR SAH & ANR.  RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This  appeal  arises  from  an  order  dated  18.09.2024
passed by the High Court of Jharkhand at Ranchi dismissing
Criminal Revision No. 864 of 2018 filed by the appellant
against the appellate order affirming his conviction under
Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 (‘N.I.
Act’).
4. By the order of the Trial Court, the appellant was
convicted under Section 138 of N.I. Act and sentenced to two
years imprisonment. In addition, he was directed to pay
compensation of Rs. 6 lakhs.  This sentence was affirmed by
the  Appellate  Court  and  the  revision  preferred  by  the
appellant was dismissed by the High Court.
5. On 09.04.2026, when the matter appeared before us, we
directed as follows:
“1. Delay condoned.
1

--- Page 638 ---
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO. 2748 OF 2026
2. The petitioner has been convicted under
Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act,
1881 and awarded sentence of two years with
fine / compensation of Rs. 6 lakhs. 
3. At the time of filing this petition, the
petitioner had moved an application seeking
exemption from surrendering pursuant to the
order  of  the  High  Court.  On  the  said
application, this Court passed an order on
04.09.2025  granting  exemption  subject  to
deposit of Rs. 4 lakhs. Admittedly, the said
amount was not deposited despite extension and,
therefore, the petitioner has been taken into
custody.
4. The learned counsel for the petitioner has
placed before us custody certificate issued by
Superintendent,  District  Jail,  Simdega
indicating that by 29.08.2025, the petitioner
had already served one year five months three
days of sentence.
5. Issue notice, returnable in six weeks.
6. In the meantime, we direct that subject to
deposit of Rs. 6 lakhs before the Registrar of
this Court, the petitioner shall be released on
bail.
7. The amount so deposited shall be invested in
an interest-bearing Fixed Term Deposit of a
Nationalized Bank initially for a period of six
months which shall be renewed thereafter from
time to time and shall abide by the result of
this petition.”
6. The learned counsel for the appellant submits that
2

--- Page 639 ---
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO. 2748 OF 2026
pursuant to the above order, the appellant has deposited the
entire compensation amount and by now he has already undergone
sentence of about one year six months.
7. Having regard to the nature of the offence; the period
of  sentence  already  undergone;  and  that  the  entire
compensation  amount  has  already  been  deposited  by  the
appellant, we deem it appropriate to modify the sentence by
reducing the sentence of imprisonment from two years to period
of imprisonment already undergone. The rest of the order as
affirmed by the High Court is hereby affirmed. 
8. The appellant, who is stated to be on bail, need not
surrender. His bail bonds are discharged. The amount deposited
by the appellant shall be released in favour of the respondent
No. 1 (the complainant).
9. The appeal stands disposed of in the above terms. 
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
                  
       .......................J.
                           (MANOJ MISRA)          
 
.......................J.
         (MANMOHAN)
 NEW DELHI 
 22nd MAY, 2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL  NO.                   OF 2026
(@ SLP(CRL.) No. 6121/2026
AJIT KUMAR JENA                  APPELLANT(S)
                      VERSUS
THE STATE OF NCT OF DELHI        RESPONDENT(S)
 
WITH
CRIMINAL APPEAL  NO.                   OF 2026
(@ SLP(Crl) No. 7402/2026)
CRIMINAL APPEAL  NO.                   OF 2026
(@ SLP(Crl) No. 9081/2026)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. We  have  heard  the  learned  Senior  Counsel
appearing for the appellants and the learned ASG
appearing for the respondent. 
3. The appellants have been arraigned as an accused
and seek regular bail in connection with FIR
No.86/2024, registered at Police Station E.O.W.,
Mandir  Marg,  New  Delhi  for  the  offences
punishable under Sections 420, 406, 467, 468,
1

--- Page 662 ---
471 and 120B of the Indian Penal Code, 1860.
4. Admittedly, the chargesheet has been filed, and
therefore, the continued incarceration of the
appellants is not required. We also take note of
the period of incarceration undergone by the
appellants.
5. In such view of the matter, we are inclined to
set aside the impugned order passed by the High
Court and grant bail to the appellants.
6. Accordingly,  the  impugned  order  stands  set
aside, and the appellants are granted bail,
subject to the terms and conditions that may be
imposed by the concerned Trial Court.
7. The appeals stand allowed, accordingly.
8. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.

  [M.M. SUNDRESH]
       ………………………………………………………………………J.
    [NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH]
 NEW DELHI;

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL  NO.                   OF 2026
(@ SLP(CRL.) No.7356/2026)
PANKAJ KUMAR MISHRA              APPELLANT(S)
                         VERSUS
THE STATE OF BIHAR              RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. We have heard the learned counsel appearing for
the appellant and the learned counsel appearing
for the respondent. 
3. The appellant seeks regular bail in connection
with FIR No.521/2024 dated 18.12.2024 registered
at Police Station– Alauli, Khagaria District for
the offences punishable under Sections 103(1),
61(1), 238, 351(3), 351(2) read with Section
3(5) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
4. Learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  appellant
1  SLP(CRL.) No.7356/2026

--- Page 666 ---
submits that it is a case of circumstantial
evidence. It is the appellant who informed the
parents of the deceased about the incident.
5. The  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the
respondent-State submitted that there are other
accused persons who have been charged along with
the appellant and the CCTV footage shows the
presence of the appellant. 
6. The appellant was working as a Guard in the
school. The alleged motive is against the other
accused  persons.  The  appellant  has  been  in
custody for more than one year and five months.
Suffice  it  is  to  state  that  continued
incarceration of the appellant is not required. 
7. Considering the above, we are inclined to set
aside the impugned order passed by the High
Court and grant bail to the appellant.
8. Accordingly,  the  impugned  order  stands  set
aside,  and  the  appellant  is  granted  bail,
subject to the terms and conditions that may be
imposed by the concerned Trial Court.
9. The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
2  SLP(CRL.) No.7356/2026

--- Page 667 ---
10. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.   
    
  ……………………………………………………………………J.
  [M.M. SUNDRESH]
       ………………………………………………………………………J.
    [NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH]
 NEW DELHI;
 25th May, 2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL)  NO.2209/2025
JYOTSNARANI HATI                              Petitioner(s)
                          VERSUS
LAKSHAY JINDAL                                Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. The parties have also appeared through virtual mode.
3. Through this petition, the petitioner – wife seeks
transfer of proceedings pending as HMA No.83/2025 pending
before Principal Judge, Family Court, Kurukshetra, Haryana
to a court of competent jurisdiction Family Court, Jajpur,
Odisha.
4. Vide order dated 24.09.2025, at the request of learned
counsel for the parties, the matter was sent to the
Mediation Centre to explore a settlement. The Mediation
Centre  of  this  Court  has  submitted  a  report  dated
23.02.2026 annexing therewith a Settlement Agreement dated
21.02.2026 arrived between the parties.
1

--- Page 670 ---
5. In  terms  of  the  Settlement  Agreement  a  sum  of
Rs.15,00,000/- (Rupees Fifteen Lakhs) was to be paid by the
second  party  (the  respondent)  towards  full  and  final
settlement of all claims arising out of the matrimonial
relationship between the parties. The petitioner accepts
that the aforesaid amount has already been paid to her.
The  settlement  agreement  also  provide  for  return  of
jewellery articles which parties agree to abide.
6. In such circumstances, the learned counsel for the
parties have prayed that in terms of paragraph 5 of the
Settlement Agreement a decree of divorce by mutual consent
be passed by this Court.
7. Having regard to the fact that parties have arrived at
an amicable settlement and they are willing to part ways by
mutual consent, we are of the view that it is a fit case
for invocation of our jurisdiction under Article 142 of the
Constitution of India to dissolve the marriage by mutual
consent in terms of the settlement.
8. Accordingly, this transfer petition stands disposed of
in terms of the settlement agreement.  
9. Let a decree of dissolution of marriage be drawn in
the aforesaid terms. The Settlement Agreement shall be part
of the decree.
2

--- Page 671 ---
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Criminal Appeal  No(s).  2800/2026
SLP (Crl.) No(s). 6910/2026
X JUVENILE                                         Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF UTTAR PRADESH & ORS.                      Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard  learned  counsel  for  the
parties.
3. This  appeal  arises  from  an  order
dated 13.01.2026 passed by the High Court of
Judicature at Allahabad in Criminal Revision
No.  6722/2025  by  which  the  revision
application of the appellant against the
order  of  the  Additional  District  and
Sessions Judge rejecting his bail prayer in
Case Crime No. 51/2025 registered at P.S.
1

--- Page 678 ---
Ata, District Jalam has been rejected. 
4. The  submission  on  behalf  of  the
appellant is that admittedly the appellant
is a juvenile aged below 18 years. The
victim was a schoolmate. The radiological
age of the victim is stated to be 18 years.
There  are  no  signs  of  violence  in  the
medical report. In such circumstances, the
appellant  ought  to  have  been  given  the
benefit of bail.
5. The  learned  Counsel  for  the  State-
respondent submits that there is a report of
the District Probation Officer that there
are chances of the appellant falling in bad
company.
6. In respect to the above, the learned
counsel for the appellant submits that the
victim must have already left the school
where the appellant was placed at the time
the alleged incident occurred. Besides, the
appellant  may  not  appear  again  for  his
examination from the same school.
7. Be that as it may, having regard to the
2

--- Page 679 ---
age of the appellant as also the medical
report of the victim, without expressing any
opinion on the merits of the case, while
taking into consideration that the appellant
is a juvenile, we are of the view that the
appellant is entitled to be released on bail
during the course of the inquiry/ trial. 
8. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed. The
order passed by the High Court is set aside.
9.The appellant shall be released on bail on
such  terms  and  conditions  as  the  trial
court/Board may deem fit to impose in the
facts and circumstances of the case.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall
stand disposed of.
  
………………………………………………………………..J
[MANOJ MISRA]
………………………………………………………………..J
[MANMOHAN]

--- Page 685 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
        CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
  CIVIL APPEAL NO.        OF 2025 
(Arising out of SLP(C) No.27719 of 2025 )
 VINERGY INTERNATIONAL PVT. LTD      Appellant(s)
                              VERSUS
    BHARAT PETROLEUM 
CORPORATION LTD. & ANR.      Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant is aggrieved by the order dated
10.07.2025 passed by the High Court of Judicature
at Bombay in exercise of power under Section 11 of
the  Arbitration  and  Conciliation  Act,  1996
(hereinafter, referred to as ‘the Act’).
3. Corporate Insolvency Resolution process was
initiated  against  Vinergy  International  Private
Limited  on  09.02.2023  and  the  Resolution  Plan
submitted by the Resolution Applicant was approved
by the National Company Law Tribunal, Mumbai, on
09.12.2024.
4. Respondent  No.1,  the  Bharat  Petroleum
1

--- Page 686 ---
Corporation (for short,  ‘the Corporation’), filed
the subject application under Section 11 of the Act
prior to the initiation of the Corporate Insolvency
Resolution Process but the same came to be decided
only after the Successful Resolution Applicant was
put in place, pursuant to the approval of the
Resolution Plan on 09.12.2024.
5. In that view of the matter, the Successful
Resolution Applicant, who stepped into the shoes of
the Corporate Debtor, cannot be fastened with the
past liabilities of the corporate debtor which did
not form part of the approved resolution plan, in
the light of the ‘clean slate’ principle applicable
to it.
6. This  being  the  legal  position,  the
Corporation  very  fairly  stated  in  its  counter
affidavit that, given the facts and circumstances
of  this  case,  the  Successful  Resolution
Applicant/Corporate Debtor may be granted relief.
7. In the light of the aforestated facts and
circumstances, we deem it appropriate to exercise
our  jurisdiction  under  Article  142  of  the
Constitution of India and limit the applicability
2

--- Page 687 ---
of the order dated 10.07.2025 passed by the High
Court  of  Judicature  at  Bombay,  appointing  an
Arbitrator, only to respondent No.2 herein.
8. We may note that respondent No. 2, despite
service  of  notice,  did  not  choose  to  enter
appearance before this Court.
9. The  appeal  is  allowed  in  the  aforestated
terms. 
10. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  stand(s)
disposed of.
   
….........................J
   (SANJAY KUMAR)
       ...........................J
   (ALOK ARADHE)
   New Delhi
   December 15, 2025 
3

Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No.9488/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  24-03-2026
in CRAS No. 3224/2025 passed by the High Court of Punjab & Haryana 
at Chandigarh]
SUMAN LATA                                         Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF HARYANA & ANR.                            Respondent(s)
(FOR ADMISSION AND IA No. 156258/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJAY KAROL
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AUGUSTINE GEORGE MASIH
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Nishant Verma, AOR
                   Mr. Ashish Ranjan, Adv.  
                 
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Samar Vijay Singh, AOR 
      UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The petitioner challenges the judgment and order dated
24-03-2026 in CRAS No.3224/2025 (O&M) titled “ Suman Lata vs.
State of Haryana & Anr. ” passed by the High Court of Punjab &
Haryana at Chandigarh.
2. Issue notice, returnable on 17.07.2026.
3. Mr.  Samar  Vijay  Singh,  learned  Advocate-on-Record,
accepts notice on behalf of the State of Haryana.  Hence, the
formal service of notice on the respondent-State is waived.
Let notice be issued to respondent No.2.
4. Dasti service, in addition, is permitted. Let steps for
1

--- Page 693 ---
service be taken within one week.
5. In  the  notice  itself,  let  it  be  mentioned  that
respondent is required to file a counter affidavit and reply
to the interlocutory application(s), if any, before the next
date of listing.
6. Learned counsel for the petitioner to serve a copy of the
present petition to the learned counsel for the respondent-
State within a period of one week. 
7. Let  counter  affidavit  be  filed  within  two  weeks.
Rejoinder  thereto,  if  any,  be  filed  within  one  week
thereafter.
8. In  the  event  of  arrest  in  connection  with  F.I.R.
No.231/2025  dated  20.08.2025,  registered  at  P.S.  Beri,
District-Jhajjar, the petitioner shall be released on bail by
the  Investigating/Arresting  Officer  on  such  terms  and
conditions  as  imposed  and  found  to  be  just,  fair  and
reasonable.
9. However, in the attending facts, one of the conditions
shall  necessarily  be  that  if  the  petitioner  possesses  a
Passport, the same shall be deposited before the Competent
Authority/Court, until the conclusion of the Trial or unless
directed otherwise. 
10. The petitioner shall make himself available before the
Investigating Officer on 01.06.2026 at 10:00 a.m. and on all
such dates as he may be required.
2

--- Page 694 ---
11.  Needless to add, till the investigation is not complete in
all respects, the petitioner would fully cooperate, and if the
challan/charge sheet is filed, he would maintain good conduct
and not attempt to influence any of the witnesses in any manner
till the completion of the trial.

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) Diary No(s). 17593/2026
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated 09-12-2025 in 1A
No. 33377/2024 in CRA No.10969/2022 passed by the High Court of
Madhya Pradesh Principal Seat at Jabalpur]
RAVI @ RISHIKESH MISHRA                            Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH                            Respondent(s)
(IA No. 158216/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 158218/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN REFILING /  CURING THE
DEFECTS
IA No. 158217/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 26-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIPUL M. PANCHOLI
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Himanshu Shekhar, Adv.
                   Mr. Rajiv Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Sandeep Kumar Pathak, Adv.
                   Mr. Kisalaya Shukla, AOR
                   Mr. Krishna Kant Shukla, Adv.
                   Mr. Subodh Kumar, Adv.
                   Ms. Shruti Mishra, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
                   Mr. Surjeet Singh,Adv.
                   Mr. Harmeet S.Ruprah, AOR
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard  Mr.  Himanshu  Shekhar,  learned  counsel
appearing for the petitioner and Mr. Surjeet Singh,
learned counsel appearing for the respondent-State, on
advance notice.
SLP (Criminal) Diary No.17593/2026 Page 1 of 3

--- Page 699 ---
2. Delay condoned.
3. The  present  petition  calls  in  question  the
correctness of the order dated 9 th December, 2025 passed
by the High Court of Madhya Pradesh at Jabalpur in IA
No.33377 of 2024 in Criminal Appeal No.10969 of 2022.
4. By the said order, the High Court has declined the
benefit of suspension of sentence and enlargement of bail
to the petitioner.  The petitioner stands convicted for
the offences punishable under Sections 376(1) read with
34 and 506 Part II of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 and
Sections 66(E) and 67(A) of the Information Technology
Act, 2000 and sentenced to undergo Rigorous Imprisonment
for 10 years along with appropriate sentence for fine and
default stipulations.
5. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that out
of  fixed  sentence  of  10  years,  the  petitioner  has
undergone 5 years and 8 months.  Learned counsel also
contends that even according to the prosecution, the
sexual assault was committed by the juvenile co-accused.
The only allegation attributed to him is of the act of
filming the deed.  Learned counsel further submits that
the Criminal Appeal filed by the petitioner before the
High Court is of the year 2022 and is likely to take some
time for disposal.
SLP (Criminal) Diary No.17593/2026 Page 2 of 3

--- Page 700 ---
6. Mr. Surjeet Singh, learned counsel appearing for
the respondent-State vehemently opposes the prayer for
suspension of sentence.
7. Considering the overall facts and circumstances and
the fact that the petitioner has undergone 5 years and 8
months, out of fixed sentence of 10 years and keeping in
mind the fact that the criminal appeal is likely to take
some time for hearing, we are inclined to suspend the
sentence and enlarge the petitioner on bail, pending
disposal of the criminal appeal by the High Court.
8. We direct that pending hearing of Criminal Appeal
No.109679 of 2022, the sentence of the petitioner is
suspended and the petitioner is enlarged on bail, subject
to the satisfaction of the Trial Court.
9. In view of the above, the Special Leave Petition is
disposed of.
10. Pending application shall also stand disposed of.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                             (KOMAL)
   AR-CUM-PS                              COURT MASTER
SLP (Criminal) Diary No.17593/2026 Page 3 of 3

--- Page 701 ---

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                        O R D E R
After  the  detailed  order  was
dictated/pronounced  in open Court which is placed on
the file to be listed on 22 nd  July, 2026, Shri Nikhil
Goel, learned senior counsel and  amicus  curiae
assisted by Ms. Rupali Samuel, learned advocate, has
brought to  the attention  of this  Court, a  news
article published in the Hindustan Times newspaper on
May 25,  2026, which  indicates that  in spite  of
directions  issued  by  this  Court  in  the  earlier
orders, the illegal sand mining and transportation
activities continue unabated. The report refers to
numerous  instances of  unregistered and unnumbered
vehicles carrying sand moving in the adjoining  areas
near the river banks. Most of these activities have
been noticed in the villages of Morena District,
Madhya Pradesh. 
 The said newspaper report is taken on record.
 Mr. S.V. Raju, learned ASG representing the
State of Madhya, shall file urgent response to the
observations noted in the aforesaid news report.
 List again on 29 th May, 2026 for the response
of State of Madhya Pradesh.
(SONIA BHASIN)
ASSISTANT REGISTRAR-CUM-PS
(NIKITA SINGH)
COURT MASTER (NSH)
(Signed Reportable order is placed on the file)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO.2821 OF 2025
APEKSHA SHARMA                       PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
PRAMOD KUMAR                       RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. This petition has been filed by the petitioner-wife
seeking transfer of H.M.A. No.2568 of 2025 titled "Pramod Kumar
vs. Apeksha Sharma”, pending before the Principal Judge, Family
Court, South West District, Dwarka Courts, New Delhi to the
Principal Judge, Family Court at Gwalior District Court, Madhya
Pradesh.
2. By this Court’s order dated 30th January, 2026, the matter
was referred for mediation. As per the report received from the
Supreme Court Mediation Centre, the mediation has failed.
3. Having heard learned counsel appearing for the parties,
we  are  inclined  to  transfer  the  case  sought  for.  We,
accordingly,  allow  the  transfer  petition  and  direct  that
H.M.A.  No.2568  of  2025  titled  "Pramod  Kumar  vs.  Apeksha
Sharma”, pending before the Principal Judge, Family Court,
South  West  District,  Dwarka  Courts,  New  Delhi  shall  be
transferred to the Principal Judge, Family Court at Gwalior
District Court, Madhya Pradesh.
1

--- Page 720 ---
4. However, if the video conferencing facility is available
with the Court, the benefit of the same shall be extended to
the parties, unless their physical presence is required.
5. The transferor Court shall forthwith transmit the record
of the aforesaid case to the transferee court.
6. The transferee Court shall make all possible endeavours
first for an amicable settlement between the parties, failing
which for early decision in the matter without unnecessary
adjournments.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
........................J.
[K.V. VISWANATHAN]  
 ........................J.
     [VIJAY BISHNOI]  
NEW DELHI;
May 20, 2026.
2

                                VERSUS
ASHISH SHANDIL                       RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. This petition has been filed by the petitioner-wife
seeking transfer of H.M.A. No.221 of 2025 titled  "Ashish
Shandil vs. Swati Thakur”, pending before the Principal Judge,
Family Court, Shimla, Himachal Pradesh to the Family Court at
Karkardooma Courts Complex, Delhi.
2. The respondent-husband, although served with the notice
issued by this Court, yet has chosen not to remain present
before this Court either in-person or through an advocate.
3. Having  heard  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the
petitioner, we are inclined to transfer the case sought for.
We, accordingly, allow the transfer petition and direct that
H.M.A. No.221 of 2025 titled "Ashish Shandil vs. Swati Thakur”,
pending before the Principal Judge, Family Court, Shimla,
Himachal Pradesh shall be transferred to the Family Court, East
District, Karkardooma Courts Complex, Delhi.
1
FAMHY COURT/ COURT OF COMPETENTJURISDICTION AT KARKARDOOMA COURT, NEW DELHI.

with the Court, the benefit of the same shall be extended to
the parties, unless their physical presence is required.
5. The transferor Court shall forthwith transmit the record
of the aforesaid case to the transferee court.
6. The transferee Court shall make all possible endeavours
first for an amicable settlement between the parties, failing
which for early decision in the matter without unnecessary
adjournments.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Our order dated 09.01.2026 reads thus:-
1

--- Page 738 ---
M A No.  1660/2026 in SLP(C) No. 1596/2026
“1. Heard the learned counsel appearing for the
petitioner.
2. Application seeking permission to file the Special
Leave Petition is granted.
3 Exemption Application is allowed. 
4. Having heard the learned counsel appearing for the
petitioner and having gone through the materials on
record, we are of the view that no error not to speak
of any error of law could be said to have been meted
out by the High Court in passing the impugned order.
5. The High Court has granted time upto 31-1-2026 to
the petitioner – herein to vacate the suit land and
handover possession of the same to the respondents.
6. The only indulgence we are inclined to grant is to
extend the time upto 31-5-2026 to vacate the suit
land.
 7. We direct the petitioner – herein to file an
affidavit on oath in the form of an undertaking that
the  possession  shall  be  handed  over  to  the
respondents on or before 31-5-2026.
8. Let such an Undertaking be filed with the Registry
of this Court within a period of one week from today.
9. With the aforesaid, the Special Leave Petition
stands disposed of.
10. Pending applications, if any, also stand disposed
of.”
2. By our order referred to above, we have granted time to the
petitioner to vacate the suit land on or before 31.05.2026.
3. By this application, the applicant prays for some further
time saying that he has already started with the construction of
a new petrol pump at some other place.  We grant further three
months to vacate the premises.
4. The  petitioner  shall  hand  over  vacant  and  peaceful
2

--- Page 739 ---
M A No.  1660/2026 in SLP(C) No. 1596/2026
possession of the suit land on or before 31.08.2026.  He shall
file an undertaking on Oath with the Registry of this Court
within a period of one week from today stating that he shall
hand over vacant and peaceful possession of the suit land on or
before 31.8.2026.
5. With the aforesaid, the Miscellaneous Application stands
disposed of.
6. Pending application(s), if any, stands disposed of.
(CHANDRESH)                                     (POOJA SHARMA)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)

circumstances of the case and the period
of  incarceration  undergone  by  the
petitioner, i.e. since 06.10.2025, we are
inclined to grant bail to the petitioner.

circumstances of the case, we direct that
the petitioner be released on bail on such
terms and conditions as may be imposed by
the Trial Court in connection with FIR
No.255 of 2024 registered at Cyber Police
Station,  Commissionerate,  Jaipur,
Rajasthan.
It  goes  without  saying  that  the
petitioner will extend all co-operation
during the trial.
In the event the Trial Court or the
State  finds  that  the  petitioner  is
