ITEM NO.23               COURT NO.16               SECTION II
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No(s).21223-
21224/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  02-09-2025
in A482 No. 13749/2017 21-11-2025 in CRMRA No. 1/2025 passed by the
High Court of Judicature at Allahabad]
JYOTI PRAKASH                                      Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF UTTAR PRADESH & ORS.                      Respondent(s)
IA No. 324653/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 90287/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 74090/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 9155/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 324655/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 9152/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 21-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE N.V. ANJARIA
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Devadatt Kamat,, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Krishnam Mishra, AOR
                   Mr. Param Kumar Mishra, Adv.
                   Mr. Hruday Bajentri,, Adv.
                   Mr. Jagjeet Kumar Bhatt, Adv.
                   Ms. Nisha Thakur, Adv.
                   Mr. Chetan Wahi, Adv.
                   Mr. Ayush Mishra, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Saurabh Mishra, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Pradeep Kumar Yadav, Adv.
                   Ms. Anjale Kumari, Adv.
                   Mr. P Murugesan, Adv.
                   Mr. Tota Ram, Adv.
                   Mr. Sanjeev Malhotra, AOR
                   
                   Mr. Yasharth Kant, AOR
1

--- Page 774 ---
                   Mr. Satwik Mishra, Adv.
                   
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Admittedly,  an  order  of  status  quo  passed  on
30.08.2013 by the Civil Court in a suit between the parties
is in operation and during pendency of the suit, the
petitioner  initiated  proceedings  on  10.04.2017  under
Section 145 of The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, in
which the present impugned order has been passed by the
High Court.
2. In (2000) 4 SCC 440, this Court has laid down the
principle  as  to  the  desirability  of  initiation  and
continuation of proceedings under Section 145 of Cr.P.C.
when a civil litigation is pending between the parties in
respect of the same property.
3. The following has been observed in para  12 of the
judgment :
“The question then is whether there is any
infirmity  in  the  Order  of  the  S.D.M.
discontinuing the proceedings under Section 145
Criminal  Procedure  Code.  The  law  on  this
subject-matter has been settled by the decision
of this Court in the case of Ram Sumer Puri
Mahant vs. State of U.P. & Ors., reported in
1985 (1) S.C.C. 427. In this case it has been
held as follows :
"When a civil litigation is pending for the
property wherein the question of possession
is involved and has been adjudicated, we see
hardly any justification for initiating a
parallel criminal proceeding under Section
145 of the Code. There is no scope to doubt
or dispute the position that the decree of
the civil court is binding on the criminal
2

--- Page 775 ---
court in a matter like the one before us.
Counsel for respondents 2-5 was not in a
position to challenge the proposition that
parallel proceedings should not be permitted
to continue and in the event of a decree of
the civil court, the criminal court should
not be allowed to invoke its jurisdiction
particularly  when  possession  is  being
examined by the civil court and parties are
in a position to approach the civil court
for interim orders such as injunction or
appointment  of  receiver  for  adequate
protection of the property during pendency
of the dispute. Multiplicity of litigation
is not in the interest of the parties nor
should public time be allowed to be wasted
over  meaningless  litigation.  We  are,
therefore,  satisfied  that  parallel
proceedings should not continue."
4. In view of the above, when an order of status quo is
already in operation, there was no justification for a
proceeding under Section 145 Cr.P.C.
5. In view of the above, we dispose of the Special
Leave Petition with observation that the order of status
quo granted by the Civil Court on 13.08.2013 shall continue
to bind the parties subject to the same being modified by
the Trial Court or being interfered by the superior courts.
6. Any  observation  made  by  the  Sub  Divisional
Magistrate or by the High Court in the impugned order shall
not affect the outcome of the civil proceedings.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
  (RAHUL KUMAR)                             (AKSHAY KUMAR BHORIA)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
3

--- Page 776 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Civil) No. 12408 of 2020)
PHOOL CHAND       APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
THE ORIENTAL INDUSRANCE CO. LTD       RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Appellant was allowed compensation of Rs.1,89,500/-
by the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (for short ‘MACT’)
with interest @ 6 per cent from the date of award.
3. The  High  Court  has  enhanced  the  amount  of
compensation by Rs. 70,000/- making the total compensation
payable  to  the  claimant  at  Rs.  2,59,500/-,  however,
interest from the date of application has been awarded only
on the enhanced amount and not on the original compensation
of Rs. 1,89,500/- granted by the MACT.
4. It is pointed out and not disputed by the respondent
that the tribunal has not awarded any compensation on the
head  of  future  prospect  which  according  to  National
Insurance Company Ltd. vs. Pranay Sethi & Ors. 1, ought to
have been allowed at 40 per cent. 
5. Considering that the law on the above aspect is
1.  2017 (16) SCC 680
1

--- Page 777 ---
settled in Pranay Sethi (Supra), we are of the considered
view that appellant should have been allowed 40 per cent of
the compensation on the head of loss of income, towards
future prospect.
6. On addition of this amount of future prospect, the
total compensation for loss of income would be around Rs.
2,52,000/-.
7. Thus,  we  allow  the  Appeal  directing  that  the
appellant is entitled for total amount of compensation at
Rs.3,22,000/-, together with interest @ 6 per cent on the
entire amount from the date of application, till its actual
payment to the claimant.
8. Any amount already paid by the respondent to the
claimant shall be adjusted while paying the remaining
balance amount to the appellant-claimant. If the claimant
supply the bank details with KYC to the insurance company,
the  balance  amount  of  compensation  shall  be  remitted
directly to the bank account of the claimant.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
  
 ………………………………………………………J.
             [PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA]
   
    ………………………………………………………J.
             [N.V. ANJARIA]
NEW DELHI;
May 19, 2026
2

--- Page 778 ---
ITEM NO.43               COURT NO.15               SECTION XI
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
CIVIL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026

                             O R D E R
1. Petitioner had invoked inherent powers of the High Court to
quash the proceedings emanating from matrimonial discord on
ground that it has remained pending for over 15 years with
the prosecution witnesses not willing to turn up in the
1

--- Page 789 ---
trial. However, the High Court instead of quashing the
proceedings issued a slew of directions to ensure the
presence of the petitioner in the proceedings before the
trial court. 
2. The submission of the petitioner is that the informant has
already  remarried  and,  therefore,  continuance  of  the
impugned proceedings is nothing but abuse of the process of
court. However, instead of quashing the proceedings, the
High Court has issued directions to the petitioner for his
presence before the Court concerned and, in that process,
non-bailable  warrant  has  also  been  issued  against  the
petitioner.  
3. It  is  the  case  of  the  petitioner  that  currently  the
petitioner is residing in United States of America and
therefore could not participate in the proceedings. However,
he would be able to participate in the proceedings of the
Trial Court in the month of July 2026.
4. Issue notice both on the application for condonation of
delay as well as special leave petition, returnable in the
third week of August 2026.
5. In the meantime, the impugned order dated 01.04.2024 shall
remain stayed.  However, the petitioner - Kapil Damodar
Mansaramani shall appear before the Magistrate Court where
the matter is pending by or before 31.07.2026.

Leave granted.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing for
the parties.
Pursuant to our earlier order dated 08.05.2026,
appellant  No.1,  appellant  No.2-the  detenue  and
respondent No.6, who is the father of the detenue are
present in the Court today.
In the presence of the Court, the detenue has
clearly expressed her will to join appellant No.1.
In such view of the matter, we are inclined to
permit the detenu to join appellant No.1.
Accordingly, the impugned order stands set aside
and the detenue is at liberty to join appellant No.1.
We make it clear that respondent No.6-father of
the detenue shall not interfere, in the detenue’s
residence with appellant No.1.
In case of any disturbance or obstruction, the
official respondents are directed to provide adequate
protection to the detenue and appellant No.1.
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.

--- Page 797 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        /2026
(arising out of SLP(CRL.) NO.21363/2025)
NARINDER KAUR APPELLANT(S)
       VERSUS
STATE OF PUNJAB & ANR. RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted. 
2. The appellant, Narinder Kaur, is the wife of the deceased
victim, Harbhajan Singh. She assails the grant of regular bail
to respondent No. 2, Jasvir Kaur, her sister-in-law, who was
instrumental in the death of her husband. 
3. By order dated 09.09.2025, passed in Criminal Miscellaneous
No. M-28087/2025, while granting such relief, a learned Judge
of the High Court of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh opined
that, even if the allegations made against respondent No. 2
were true, the only role attributed to her was that she handed
over the weapon of offence to the co-accused, Kuldeep Singh,
her husband, and instructed him to end the dispute, whereupon,
he shot the deceased, Harbhajan Singh. 
4. The weapon in question was allegedly the licensed revolver of
respondent No. 2, Jasveer Kaur, and the specific allegation
against her, which is reiterated by the State of Punjab in its
1

--- Page 798 ---
counter affidavit, is that she fetched the said weapon from
the vehicle, handed it over to her husband and exhorted him to
finish the dispute. 
5. This action attributed to respondent No.2 can hardly be stated
to be less than an overt act, as has been categorized by the
High Court in the impugned order.  We, therefore, find that
the High Court was not justified in granting regular bail to
respondent No. 2. 
6. The appeal is accordingly allowed, setting aside the impugned
order dated 09.09.2025, passed by the High Court of Punjab &
Haryana  at  Chandigarh  in  Criminal  Miscellaneous  No.  M-
28087/2025. 
7. Respondent No. 2 shall surrender before the trial court within
a period of two weeks from today. 
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
..........................J.
(SANJAY KUMAR)
..........................J.
(K. VINOD CHANDRAN)
NEW DELHI;
MAY 25, 2026.
2

--- Page 799 ---

SHIVAM SHARMA                                      Petitioner(s)

STATE OF NCT OF DELHI & ANR.                       Respondent(s)
IA No. 148934/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No.9959/2026 (Item 69)
IA No. 154100/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN REFILING /  CURING THE
DEFECTS, IA No. 154101/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE
IMPUGNED JUDGMENT
 
Date : 22-05-2026 These matters were called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANOJ MISRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Ravi Prakash Mehrotra, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Shashi Ranjan, Adv.
                   Mr. Abhishek Vikas, AOR
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
SLP (Crl.)  No(s).  9034/2026
1. Through this petition, the petitioner seeks to impugn the
order of the High Court dated 30.04.2026 by which the stay
application of the petitioner has been rejected.
2. The case of the petitioner is that he has suffered two ex-
1

--- Page 801 ---
parte orders in the proceedings instituted by the second respondent
under Section 12 of the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence
Act, 2005.  It has been submitted that by an ex-parte order dated
14.12.2020, the learned Magistrate has passed protective orders in
respect of two flats when at the most it ought to have been in
respect of one flat.  It is the case of the petitioner that taking
advantage of the protective order, the second respondent has let
out a flat and, therefore, the High Court ought to have modified
the order passed by the court below.
3. As we notice that the proceedings are pending before the first
Court, the appropriate course for the petitioner is to seek for
modification  of  the  interim  orders  passed  therein  by  moving
appropriate  application.   It  is  expected  that  if  any  such
application  is  submitted  by  the  petitioner,  the  Court  shall
entertain such application and after hearing the parties pass
appropriate orders.  We also clarify that while dealing with any
such application for modification of the ex-parte order, the court
shall not be prejudice by any observations made by the Appellate
Court as well as the High Court in the orders impugned.
4. With the aforesaid observations, the special leave petition
stands disposed of.
5. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
2

--- Page 802 ---
Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No.9959/2026
1. The learned counsel for the petitioner prays to withdraw the
special leave petition.
2. The special leave petition is dismissed as withdrawn.
  (KAVITA PAHUJA)                               (SAPNA BANSAL)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL  NO.                   OF 2026
(@ SLP(CRL.) No.6009/2026)
  SHAM KAILAS UFAD                PETITIONER(S)
                        VERSUS
STATE OF MAHARASHTRA            RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. We have heard the learned counsel appearing for
the appellant and the learned counsel appearing
for the respondent. 
3. The appellant has been arraigned as an accused
and seeks regular bail in connection with FIR
No.172/2024  dated  14.06.2024,  registered  at
Police Station – Maujpuri, District - Jalna for
the offences punishable under Sections 506, 504,
307, 302, 149, 148, 147 and 143 of the Indian
Penal Code, 1860.
4. Admittedly, the trial has commenced and material
witnesses have been examined before the Trial
Court.  The  appellant  has  been  under
1  SLP(CRL.) No.6009/2026

--- Page 807 ---
incarceration  for  one  year  and  11  months,
approximately.
5. We  also  take  note  of  the  fact  that  the
complainant  in  the  present  FIR  has  been
arraigned as an accused in a cross case.
6. Considering the aforementioned circumstances, we
are inclined to set aside the impugned order
passed by the High Court and grant bail to the
appellant.
7. Accordingly,  the  impugned  order  stands  set
aside,  and  the  appellant  is  granted  bail,
subject to the terms and conditions that may be
imposed by the concerned Trial Court.
8. The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
9. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.   
    
  ……………………………………………………………………J.
  [M.M. SUNDRESH]
       ………………………………………………………………………J.
    [NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH]
 NEW DELHI;

Date : 25-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANOJ MISRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Gautam Khazanchi, Adv.
                   Mr. Samarth Krishan Luthra, Adv.
                   Mr. Sumitava Chakraborty, Adv.
                   Mr. Vaibhav Dubey, AOR
                   Mr. Manoviraj Singh, Adv.

For Respondent(s) : Mr Kunal Mimani, AOR
Mr Parag Chaturvedi, Adv. 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The  petitioner  was  accorded  interim
protection vide order dated 26.02.2026 of the High
Court passed in CRR No. 959/2026. The said interim
protection was to operate for a period of 12 weeks
1

--- Page 816 ---
from the date of the order or until further order,
whichever  is  earlier,  on  condition  that  the
petitioner shall make all efforts in co-operating
with the investigating agency as and when required.
2. The  grievance  of  the  petitioner  is  that
thereafter  the  matter  was  not  taken  up  and,
therefore,  an  application  was  submitted  for
extension of the interim order ,  but on the said
application, on 21.05.2026, an order was passed
directing the application to be listed after the
upcoming summer vacations.
3. The concern of the petitioner is that if the
interim order is not extended, the petition pending
before  the  High  Court  would  be  rendered
infructuous.
4. We  have  been  informed  that  the  summer
vacations are likely to get over by 10.06.2026.
5. In  such  circumstances,  we  deem  it
appropriate to extend the operation of the interim
order  dated  26.02.2026  up  to  15.06.2026.  Upon
reopening of the High Court after summer recess, it
shall be open for the petitioner to seek for early
hearing  of  the  pending  matter  or  for  further
extension of the interim order passed earlier.
6. With the aforesaid direction, this special
leave petition stands disposed of.
2

--- Page 817 ---
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

and order dated 13.04.2026 passed by the High Court of

No. 5201 of 2026, directing the appellant herein to
deposit  entirety  of  the  decretal  amount  of

delay to set aside the ex-parte decree.

appearing for the appellants and Mr. Siddarth Dave,
learned counsel appearing for the respondents, and
considering the matter in detail, we are of the
opinion that interest of justice would be subserved if
the appellants are directed to deposit an amount of
Rs. 15,00,000/- (Rupees Fifteen Lakhs) instead of
Rs.22,72,236/-.

Court is modified to the extent that the appellants
shall deposit Rs. 15,00,000/- (Rupees Fifteen Lakhs)

disposed of.

COMMISSIONER OF CENTRAL EXCISE                     Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
M/S EICHER TRACTOR                                 Respondent(s)
 
WITH
S.L.P.(C)...CC No. 18831/2011 (IV-C)
SLP(C) No. 35552/2013 (III-B)
Date : 21-05-2026 This appeal was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARAVIND KUMAR
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE S.V.N. BHATTI
For Appellant(s):  Mr. Raj Bahadur Yadav, AOR
                   Mr. B. Krishna Prasad, AOR
  
                   Mr. N. Venkataraman, A.S.G.
                   Mr. Gurmeet Singh Makker, AOR
                   Mr. Arijit Prasad, Adv.
                   Ms. Nisha Bagchi, Adv.
                   Mr. Raghvendra S Srivatsa, Adv.
                   Mr. Ishaan Sharma, Adv. 
  
For Respondent(s): Mr. V Lakshmikumaran, Adv.
                   Ms. Charanya Lakshmikumaran, AOR
                   Mr. Swastik Mishra, Adv.
                   Ms. Nitum Jain, Adv.
                   Ms. Neha Choudhary, Adv.
                   Mr. L Badri Narayanan, Adv.
                   Ms. Medha Sinha, Adv.
                   Mr. Atreya GC, Adv. 
    Mr. R. Parthasarathy, AOR
                   Mr. Vikas Singh Jangra, AOR 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
Civil Appeal No(s). 8873/2011
1. Ms. Nisha Bagchi, learned Senior Counsel for the  
appellant(s)  submitted  that  since  the  tax  effect  
involved  in  this  appeal  is  less  than  a  sum  of  
1

--- Page 825 ---
Rs.5,00,00,000/- as per Circular No.09/2024 dated 17 th 
September, 2024 of Ministry of Finance, Department of  
Revenue,  Central  Board  of  Direct  Taxes  (CBDT),  
Government of India, New Delhi, the appellant would not 
be required to prosecute the instant appeal.
2. Accordingly, the Civil Appeal is dismissed in view 
of the low tax effect.
Special Leave Petition(Civil) No. 18831/2011
1. It  is  stated  that  along  with  Civil  Appeal  
No.21847/2017, this matter had already been dismissed  
on the ground of low tax effect. However, from the ROPs 
of this proceeding, we do not find the said order being 
reflected.
2. As such, we reiterate that on account of low tax  
effect, the Special Leave Petition is dismissed.
Special Leave Petition(Civil) No. 35552/2013
1. The issue involved in this Special Leave Petition  
is no more res integra.
2. In view of the order of this Court referred in  
Civil Appeal No.9008 of 2011, wherein it came to be  
held as under:
“On the facts of these cases we find that the  
assessee had been rightly held entitled to  
reversal of the Cenvat Credit in respect of  
the inputs which were produced with effect  
2

--- Page 826 ---
from  01.08.2004  the  date  from  which  the  
assessee was granted exemption from payment  
of excise duty in respect of final products.  
Thus, when the reverse of Cenvat Credit was  
given to the assessee on that date, final  
product was not exempted. In view thereof,  
Rule  6(1)  of  the  Cenvat  Credit  Rules,  
2002/2004  shall  not  be  applied  since  the 
present cases are squarely covered by the  
judgment  dated  11.08.1999  passed  by  this  
Court in Collector of Central Excise, Pune  
vs. Dai Ichi Karkaria Ltd. [1999 (112) E.L.T.  
353 S.C.)].”
3. The present Special Leave Petition fails and is,  
accordingly, dismissed.
(ABHINAV KUMAR)                                 (AVGV RAMU)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
3

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Having  heard  the  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the
parties and having gone through the materials on record, we
are of the view that we should not exercise our discretion as
regards the plea for bail at this point of time as the trial
is in progress.
2. We are informed by the learned counsel appearing for the

--- Page 857 ---
2
State that 02 witnesses have been examined so far. Prosecution
intends to examine 17 witnesses.
3. We fail to understand that in this type of prosecution
why the State wants to examine 17 witnesses.
4. Be that as it may, we direct the Trial Court to see that
within a period of three months from today, the trial is
concluded.
5. The disposal of the trial shall be informed to this
Court.
6. With the aforesaid, the Special Leave Petition stands
disposed of.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed
of.

--- Page 879 ---

State within a period of one week.

thereafter.

imposed and found to be just, fair and reasonable.

shall  necessarily  be  that  if  the  petitioner  possesses  a
Passport, the same shall be deposited before the Competent
Authority/Court, until the conclusion of the Trial or unless
directed otherwise.

Investigating Officer on 01.06.2026 at 10:00 a.m. and on all
such dates as he may be required.
9.  Needless to add, till the investigation is not complete in
all respects, the petitioner would fully cooperate, and if the
challan/charge sheet is filed, he would maintain good conduct
and not attempt to influence any of the witnesses in any manner
till the completion of the trial.

2
that  the  petitioner  shall  hand  over  the  peaceful  vacant
possession of the premises to the respondent on or before
31.12.2026 and shall not part with or create third party right
therein.  A usual undertaking shall be filed within a period of
three weeks from today before the Registrar, High Court of
Madhya Pradesh, Principal Bench at Jabalpur.  Non-filing of the
undertaking shall give a cause to the landlord to execute the
decree and the time for vacation as specified above shall not
have any bearing to the execution proceedings.  Defiance of the
above terms, if any, may be treated as non-compliance of the
order of this Court.
3. This order has been passed without issuing notice to other
side, however, in case the respondent-landlord feels aggrieved
by the order granting time for vacation of the suit premises, he
is at liberty to apply before this Court.
4. The Special Leave Petition is, accordingly, dismissed with
above terms.  Pending applications, if any, shall stand disposed
of.
(GULSHAN KUMAR ARORA)                           (NAND KISHOR)
   DEPUTY REGISTRAR                          ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

2. It is a leave granted matter. The appeal is
of the year, 2026 and may take sometime before it
is finally heard.

on record, has already undergone imprisonment as
an under trial as well as as a convict for more
than ten years.

released  on  bail  subject  to  the  terms  and

including surrendering of his passport, if any,
with the Trial Court itself.

allowed.

registered  with  Police  Station  CBN  Ujjain,  District
1

--- Page 953 ---
Ujjain, Madhya Pradesh for offences punishable under

162.350 kg. of poppy straw without lancing and 35.510 kg.
lanced poppy straw.

Mohan  Rao,  the  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the

this  date,  3  witnesses  have  been  examined.   The
prosecution intends to examine 6 more witnesses.  We are
conscious of the fact that the petitioner is in judicial
custody past more than 1.5 years.
5. However, since the trial is in progress, we are not
inclined to consider the plea of the petitioner for bail.
The trial shall proceed expeditiously.  The Trial Court
shall see to it that the examination of the witnesses is
completed within a period of three months from today.
6. With the aforesaid, the Special Leave Petition is
disposed of.

                             O R D E R
Heard learned senior counsel/counsels for the parties.
2. Though,  counter  affidavits  filed  on  behalf  of  private
respondent i.e., respondent no.2 in both the petitions are on
record, but learned counsel for the petitioner submits that copy of
the same have not been served on him.

petitioner during the course of the day.
4. Learned counsel for the petitioner further submits that the
private respondent is still continuing to issue threats to the
petitioner and his family members.
5. Though,  the  same  is  denied  by  the  learned  senior
counsel/counsel for the private respondent but still, we observe
that the Court will take a strict view in the matter, if any
attempt is made, either to influence the witnesses or in any way to
intimidate the petitioner/informant and any of his relatives.

on 31.07.2026.

ITEM NO.30               COURT NO.13               SECTION XIV
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).38401/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 28-10-2025
in CMM No.1388/2019 passed by the High Court of Delhi at New Delhi]
DELHI TRANSPORT CORPORATION (DTC)                  Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
M/S ZEITGEIST TECHNOLOGIES LTD.                     Respondent(s)
(FOR ADMISSION) 
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This petition was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Shubhranshu Padhi, AoR
                   Mr. D.girish Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Jay Nirupam, Adv.
                   Mr. Pranav Giri, Adv.
                   Mr. Ekansh Sisodia, Adv.
                   Mr. Ritik Sharma, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Nitin Gaur, Adv.
                   Mr. Anshuman Ashok, AoR
                   Mr. Anshul Singh, Adv.                   
                   
         
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsels for the parties.
2. We find that the present petition is unnecessarily pending
before this Court as both the parties are not cooperating in the
execution proceeding filed by the respondent which as of now, has
stood dismissed and no final order is being passed on a petition

--- Page 1025 ---
2
filed by the respondent for restoration of the said execution
proceeding. Thus, the Court was of the opinion that the present
matter needs to be closed as for all practical purposes, it has
become infructuous.
3. At this juncture, learned counsel for the respondent prays for
one last indulgence to await final decision of the petition for
restoration of the execution proceeding.

2
3. Basically, the matter relates to non-implementation of an

Industrial Tribunal, Mumbai in Reference (IT) No.81 of 2005.

implementation of the award dated 22.03.2021 in its entirety by
making full and final payment.

to be listed on 14.07.2026 at 03:30 p.m., treating it as part-
heard.

of listing, full compliance is not made and an affidavit filed in
this regard, the Court shall proceed with initiating proceeding
against the applicant/petitioner-Corporation for contempt of this
Court. Parties to come with the name of the person holding the
post.

                   Mr. Yogesh Vats, Adv.
                   Ms. Manju Jetley, AOR
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Learned counsel for the petitioner would submit that in a
similar  situation  as  of  that  of  the  petitioner  herein,  the
respondent no. 1 had selected a candidate by name Sh. Chander
Prakash for the post of TGT (Drawing) vide memorandum dated
02.08.2011, whereunder he was holding very same four years’ diploma
in  Applied  Art  awarded  by  the  institute  i.e.  Shilpa  Bharti
1

--- Page 1065 ---
Institute Of Fine Arts & Crafts, New Delhi and the Government of
Rajasthan had specifically clarified vide Department of Education
Letter Dated 28.07.1997 and 22.07.1993 addressed to the Director,
Shilpa Bharti Institute Of Fine Arts, South Patel Nagar, Delhi that
four years’ diploma in Applied Arts after 10+2 is recognized as
equivalent  to  five  years’  degree  diploma  after  tenth  class
examination.
2. The learned counsel for the petitioner would also fairly
admit that the extant rules governing has since been changed.
3. In that view of the matter, we issue notice to respondents
to ascertain the factual position, returnable in eight weeks.
(DEEPANSHU)                                     (AVGV RAMU)
SENIOR PERSONAL ASSISTANT                     COURT MASTER (NSH)

        UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
In terms of the signed reportable judgment, the
appeals are partly allowed,  the conviction of the
appellant as recorded by the trial Court and affirmed
by the High Court, is upheld. The sentences imposed in
respect of offences punishable under sections 392, 436,
506 and 411 of the IPC and Section 25/4 of the Arms Act
are also affirmed. 
1

--- Page 1103 ---
However, the sentence of death awarded for the
offence punishable under Section 302 of the IPC is
commuted to imprisonment for life which shall enure for
a  fixed  term  of  25  (twenty-five)  years  without
remission. The appellant shall also pay a fine of
Rs.25,000/-  (Rupees  Twenty-Five  Thousand  only);  in
default of payment of fine, he shall undergo further
simple imprisonment for a period of six months.
Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO(S).       OF 2026
   [@ D.NO. 21755 OF 2026]
COMMISSIONER OF CUSTOMS                            Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
M/S HEMOGENOMICS PVT LTD.                          Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. There is an enormous delay of 187 days in filing the Civil
Appeal(s). The application for condonation of delay in filing the
Civil Appeal does not give sufficient cause. 
2. Even otherwise, having heard learned counsel appearing for the
appellant(s) and having considered the facts and circumstances of
the case(s), we are of the opinion that the Customs, Excise &
Service Tax Appellate Tribunal has not committed any error in law
or fact. 
3. The Civil Appeal is accordingly  dismissed on the ground of
delay as well as on merits.  
      …………………………………………………………………………J.
   [PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA]
     …………………………………………………………………………J.
   [ALOK ARADHE]

1. By order dated 13.01.2026 notice was issued to the
respondent-wife for the limited purpose of exploring a
settlement between the parties.
2. On service of notice, the wife is represented.
3. From the submissions of the learned counsel for the
respondent it does not appear that there is possibility of
a settlement.
4. In  such  circumstances,  the  transfer  petition  is
dismissed.
5. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
…………………………………………………J
    [MANOJ MISRA]
……………………………………………………J
    [MANMOHAN]
New Delhi
MAY 21, 2026

1. Refiling delay condoned.
2. The special leave petition is reported to be beyond time by

prosecution case is supported by eye-witness account which is
1

--- Page 1163 ---
corroborated by medical evidence, we do not find a good reason to
interfere with the concurrent findings of the two courts below.

the ground of delay as well as merits.

of the petitioner to seek for remission of his sentence as per the
State Policy.

ITEM NO.32               COURT NO.14               SECTION II-C
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) Diary No(s).22040/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 10-06-2025
in CRLA No.480/2022 passed by the High Court of Chhatisgarh at
Bilaspur]
SHEIKH RAHMAN                                      Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF CHHATTISGARH                              Respondent(s)
IA No. 152488/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 152495/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 152493/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 22-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARAVIND KUMAR
         HON'BLE  MR. JUSTICE PRASANNA B. VARALE
For Petitioner(s) : Ms. Kumud Lata Das, AOR
                   Mr. Harsh Ajay Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Pooja Rathore, Adv.
                   Mr. Ashutosh Saini, Adv.
                   Mr. Puneet, Adv.                               
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Delay condoned. 
3. This is a legal aid matter and petitioner is represented
through Panel Advocate of Supreme court Legal Services Committee. 
4. For examining as to why the sentence should not be enhanced,
we issue notice to petitioner in the light of the observation made
by the Additional Sessions Judge, Fast-Track Special Court vide
judgment dated 03.01.2022 in paragraph No.39 which is to the
following effect:
“Section 42 of POCSO Act, does not include s. 377 of the
IPC. In such a situation, the accused will be sentenced
separately for the punishment provided for s. 377 of IPC
1

--- Page 1199 ---
read-with s. 511 and read-with s.  6 read-with s. 18 of
the POCSO Act. s. 377 of IPC provides for a maximum
sentence of Life Imprisonment and a minimum of 10 years
imprisonment. Similarly, s. 6 of the POCSO Act provides
for a maximum sentence of death or alternatively Life
Imprisonment  and  a  minimum  of  20  years  Rigorous
Imprisonment”.
5. Trial Court also having noticed in paragraph No.38 that POCSO
Act has been amended with effect from 16.08.2019 and the offence in
question had occurred on 31.10.2019, i.e., subsequent to the
amendment, while sentencing prima facie seems to have committed a
serious error as is evident from the impugned judgments. Hence, for
examining as to why sentence should not be enhanced, we have
ordered notice to the petitioner and the respondent – State will
also be heard. Insofar as merits canvassed by the learned counsel
appearing for the petitioner challenging the judgment on various
grounds, we do not find any good ground to entertain the petition.
Hence, the said prayer stands rejected.
6. At this juncture, learned counsel appearing for the petitioner
seeks leave of the Court to withdraw the petition. However, in view
of the observation made hereinabove, we do not deem it just and
proper to permit the petitioner to withdraw the petition. Hence,
prayer stands rejected.
7. Issue notice to the petitioner through jurisdictional SHO /
Superintendent of Central Jail, Durg (CG), Chhattisgarh since the
petitioner is said to be undergoing the sentence, returnable within
eight (08) weeks.  
  (NEHA GUPTA)                                   (AVGV RAMU)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
2

--- Page 1200 ---

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 15-05-2026
in WPL No. 16463/2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Bombay]
SAHAKAR GLOBAL LTD.                                Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
MAHARASHTRA STATE ROAD DEVELOPMENT CORPORATION 
LTD. MAHARASHTRA STATE ROAD DEVELOPMENT 
CORPORATION LTD. & ORS.   Respondent(s)
IA No. 156274/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED
JUDGMENT
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE THE CHIEF JUSTICE
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE JOYMALYA BAGCHI
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIPUL M. PANCHOLI
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Mukul Rohatgi, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Balbir Singh, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Mukesh Butani, Adv.
                   Ms. Ranjeeta Rohatgi, Adv.
                   Mr. Shankey Agrawal, AOR
                   Mr. Harsh Shukla, Adv.
                   Mr. Siddharth Agrawal, Adv.
                   Mr. Nitin Dhatarwal, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Shyam Divan, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Nikilesh Ramachandran, AOR
                   Mr. Shubham Seth, Adv.
                   Mr. Jayendra Kapadia, Adv.
                   Mr. Tapan Agarwal, Adv.
                   Mr. Jui Parekh, Adv.
                   Mr. Aditya Krishnan, Adv.
                                      
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The instant special leave petition is directed against an
interim order dated 15.05.2026 passed by a Vacation Bench of the
Bombay High Court, in terms whereof, the interim stay on the tender
1

--- Page 1213 ---
cancellation corrigendum dated 29.04.2026 has been declined. We
find the interest of the petitioner has been safeguarded with the
twin conditions that the petitioner may participate in the fresh
tender process without prejudice to its rights and that the fresh
tender floated by the first and second respondents on 29.04.2026
shall be subject to the outcome of the writ petition.
2. The matter is now listed before the High Court on 08.06.2026.
3. We have heard Shri Mukul Rohatgi, learned Senior Counsel, on
behalf of the petitioner and Shri Shyam Divan, learned Senior
Counsel, on behalf of the Maharashtra State Road Development
Corporation. We are informed that the last date of submission of a
fresh tender is 29.05.2026.
4. Having regard to all the attending circumstances, we direct
the Maharashtra State Road Development Corporation to finalise the
fresh tender subject to the conditions imposed by the High Court by
08.06.2026 and submit a report in a sealed cover before the High
Court. The follow-up action will depend upon the outcome of those
proceedings.
5. The Special Leave Petition is, accordingly, disposed of.
Pending applications, if any, stand closed.
  
(ARJUN BISHT)                                (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
2

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  12-11-2024
in WA No. 2974/2024 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Madras]
P. THARABAI                                        Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE UNION OF INDIA & ORS.                          Respondent(s)
IA No. 156495/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 156493/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 26-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE N.V. ANJARIA
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Km Vignesh Ram, Adv.
                   Mr. Naveen Raj, Adv.
                   Mr. Sarath S Janardanan, AOR
                   Ms. Vishnupriya P Govind, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits
that the High Court is not correct in observing
that the earlier Supreme Court order passed in
Civil Appeal No. 5677 of 1994, is not a binding
precedent and the relief therein was granted to
the employees only as one time measure. According

--- Page 1225 ---
2
to him, petitioner’s intervention application was
allowed by this Court in the case of Government of
India  and  Others  Versus  Court  Liquidator’s
Employees Assn. And Others ((1999) 8 Supreme Court
Cases 560) wherein, Civil Appeal No. 5677 of 1994
was also decided.
3. Thus, according to him, petitioner is entitled
to the relief granted by this Court and the High
Court has mistakenly construed that the petitioner
is not a party in that proceedings.
4. Issue notice, returnable in eight weeks.
         (NISHA KHULBEY)                    (AKSHAY KUMAR BHORIA)
         COURT MASTER(SH)                     COURT MASTER(NSH)

         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE N.V. ANJARIA
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Puneet Singh Bindra, AOR
                   Mr. R K Kapoor, Adv.
                   Ms. Aakriti Kapila, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
           UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits
that  the  Labour  Court  directed  petitioner’s
reinstatement with full back wages and continuity
of service from 15.05.1989 and the Writ Petition
preferred by the respondent remained pending for
about  19  years.  The  High  Court,  instead  of
deciding the matter on merits, impliedly held that
the petitioner is entitled for reinstatement but

--- Page 1229 ---
2
instead of directing reinstatement, the High Court
granted relief of compensation to the petitioner.
However, the same has been limited to a sum of
Rs.5,00,000/- which the respondent had deposited
in the High Court during the pendency of the Writ
Petition.
3. According  to  the  learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner, as per the order of the Labour Court,
petitioner is entitled for back wages to the tune
of Rs.97,00,000/- and after deducting the amount
of Rs.29,83,049.55/-(which the petitioner earned
while gainfully employed), the amount would come
to Rs.67,94,332/-. As against this, the High Court
has  granted  compensation  of  paltry  amount  of
Rs.5,00,000/- along with interest.
4. Issue notice, returnable in eight weeks.
         (NISHA KHULBEY)                    (AKSHAY KUMAR BHORIA)
         COURT MASTER(SH)                     COURT MASTER(NSH)

Vacation Bench of the High Court. 
The Special leave Petition is dismissed as not pressed with
the aforesaid liberty.

Ms.  Nandita  Rao,  learned  senior  counsel
appearing  for  the  petitioner  submitted  that  the

1

seeking release of the passport.

the next date fixed before the  High Court is  15 th
June, 2026.

learned  senior  counsel,  the  petitioner  is  given
liberty  to seek appropriate directions  before the

High Court may consider the request made by the
petitioner, in accordance to law.

application(s) shall stand disposed of accordingly.

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).  12002/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  03-12-2025
in WA No. 1794/2014 passed by the High Court of Karnataka at 
Bengaluru]
SOMASCAN SOCIETY                                   Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
CHINNAVENKATAMMA & ORS.                            Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION 
IA No. 130142/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 130141/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANOJ MISRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Shailesh Madiyal, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Mahesh Thakur, AOR
                   Mr. Swaroop Srinivas, Adv.
                   Mr. Narveer Yadav, Adv.
                   Ms. Ruchi Kumari, Adv.
                   Mr. Anchit Singla, Adv.
                   Mr. Siddhartha Sati, Adv.
                   Dr. Anthony Raju, Adv.
                   Mr. Dushyant Pratap Singh, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. While  dismissing  the  writ  appeal  of  the
petitioner, the High Court has left it open for the
petitioner to take recourse to the remedy of civil
1

--- Page 1343 ---
suit  for  asserting  his  right  over  the  land  in
question.  In  such  circumstances,  we  decline  to
entertain this petition.  
2. However, the apprehension of the petitioner is
that  there  are  adverse  findings  returned  by  the
learned Single Judge which may prejudicially affect
petitioner’s right.
3. Be that as it may, it is well settled that in
summary  proceedings  title  to  the  land  is  not
determined  and,  therefore,  any  findings  returned
during these summary proceedings are not binding on
the civil court in regular title suit.
4. We, therefore, dispose of this petition by
giving liberty to the petitioner to take recourse to
civil law remedy. It is made clear that we have not
expressed any opinion on the rights of the parties qua
the property in dispute.
5. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.      
(CHETAN ARORA)                                  (SAPNA BANSAL)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
2

--- Page 1344 ---

Birthday celebration at Jaipur)
3. 28th June to 3rd July – Overnight stay at any
hotel in Jaipur, mother will stay in a separate
room in the same property.
4. 4th July and onwards – child will be with
Mother, as earlier.”

Mr. Dere Kishor Shankar, Adv.
                    By Courts Motion, AOR                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Miscellaneous  Application  (MA 1603/2026)  is  disposed of granting one
year’s time to the trial judge to conclude the trial in terms of the earlier order of
this Court dated 11th November, 2024.
2. Since  we  have  found  from  the  report  of  the  trial  court  that  the  key
witnesses  are  being  threatened  and/  or  intimidated,  we  direct  the
Superintendent  of  Police,  Amroha  to  ensure  the  safety and  security  of  the
witnesses of fact who, as per the report, are shy to testify.
3. All such witnesses of fact may be provided with police protection to join
the proceedings before the trial court and testify.
(JATINDER KAUR)                               (SUDHIR KUMAR SHARMA)
P.S. to REGISTRAR                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

                   Mr. Fateh Sahota, Adv.                   
For Respondent(s) : 
Mr. Siddhant Sharma, Adv.
         UPON hearing the counsel, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
We find that the mother of the deceased, who was a party to
the proceedings before the High Court, has conveniently been left
out from the array of parties in this special leave petition.
That apart, even on merits, we find no grounds made out to
interfere with the impugned order passed by the High Court, denying
anticipatory bail to the petitioners.
The special leave petition is, accordingly, dismissed.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

                    Mr. Punit Agarwwal, Adv.
                    Mr. Sajal Jain, AOR                            
For Respondent(s) : 
   UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. While we are not inclined to interfere with the impugned order
passed by the High Court, it is necessary to clarify that the High
Court has declared that the Ph.D. conferred on the petitioner to be
void.  There is no such direction as regards the graduate or post-
